docs.title: Meal Prep
docs.text: A solo vegetarian meal prep system for one morning cook covering lunch + dinner — built for Hyderabad weather, a 12-9 workday, and a 2-hour weekend prep session.

docs.title: Start here
docs.text:
• How the System Works — the two-layer model and the three non-negotiables
  • Weekly Variations — 5 rotatable weeks; pick by protein/effort/freezer mood
  • Storage Cheat Sheet — what lasts how long, fridge vs freezer
  • Shopping Lists · Freezer Guide

docs.title: Recipes
docs.text:
Weekend prep components (make once, use all week): masala base, boiled legumes, boiled dal + tadka, boiled potatoes, veggie boxes, curd, sprouts, rotis/theplas, sambar-style dal.

Weekday dishes (25-45 min, morning): rajma/chole from components, soya bhurji, matar paneer, lauki kootu, pav bhaji, kadai vegetable, dalia pulao, pongal.

Browse the full list in the Recipes section of the nav. Every recipe states its fridge life, freezer-safety, and active time up top.

docs.title: Freezer Portioning Guide
docs.text:
• Freeze in single-meal portions — you'll never defrost more than you need.
  • Cool cooked food first, but don't leave it sitting out for hours. Move to fridge/freezer once steam has stopped and food is warm, not hot.
  • Use flat/shallow containers; they chill, freeze, and thaw faster than deep boxes.
  • Leave a little headspace for dals, legumes, and sambar-dal because liquids expand slightly when frozen.
  • Label with masking tape + marker: item + date. (Sounds fussy; saves you from mystery boxes by Thursday.)
  • Thawing without a microwave: move tomorrow's portion from freezer → fridge the night before, or drop the frozen block straight into the kadai on low with a splash of water and reheat till piping hot. 🔲 With a microwave: defrost 2-3 min, then reheat till fully hot.
  • Freezer-safe: legumes, dals, masala base, sambar-dal, theplas. Not freezer-safe: boiled potato, curd, cooked rice, cut raw veggies (except the ones you bought pre-frozen).

docs.title: One-time buys
docs.text:
Containers

  • 4-6 pc borosilicate glass airtight set, square/rectangular, 500 ml-1 L (Borosil / Cello Ornella / Femora, ~₹1,200-2,000). Fridge + freezer + microwave safe.
  • 3-4 small 200-300 ml containers or a silicone cube tray — for single-meal frozen masala-base portions.
  • Masking tape + marker for freezer labels.
  • Repurpose: steel dabbas → chopped veg & potatoes; ice-cream/curd tubs → dry storage and fridge organizers only (never hot food, never reheating).

Microwave (recommended): 23-25 L solo (LG / Samsung / IFB, ~₹7,000-10,000). Solo, not convection — the 60L OTG already covers baking/grilling. Sized for a family of 4 later.

docs.title: Weekly groceries
docs.text: Every week: onions 1 kg · tomatoes 1 kg · green chilli / ginger / garlic · milk for curd · 1-2 packs idli batter · lemon Rotating: 2 hardy veggies (lauki/kaddu/gobi/gawar) + 1 delicate (bhindi/toru — eat in the first half of the week, or midweek Zepto top-up) High-protein weeks (V4): paneer 200 g bought fresh Fri/Sat · soya chunks/granules (pantry, restock monthly) Optional if bought that week: capsicum (keeps 4-5 days)

docs.title: Pantry check (monthly)
docs.text: Dals · rajma / chole / chana / moth · rice · atta · besan · daliya · poha · mangodi · soya chunks · sambar powder · tamarind · kasuri methi · pav bhaji masala · frozen peas / carrot / beans restock

docs.title: Storage & Safety Cheat Sheet
docs.text:
Item Fridge Freezer Onion-tomato masala base 4 days 3-4 weeks Boiled legumes (in liquid) 3 days 2-3 weeks Plain boiled dal 3 days 2-3 weeks Cooked gravy dishes (rajma/chole) 2-3 days 2 weeks Sambar-style dal 3 days 2 weeks Cooked dry sabzi 2-3 days ❌ Cooked rice 1-2 days (but just cook fresh) ❌ Cooked rotis 2-3 days ❌ (theplas: 1 week ✅) Boiled potatoes (skin on) 3-4 days ❌ Chopped raw veggies (dry, airtight) 3-4 days ❌ Idli/dosa batter (readymade) 3-4 days ❌ Home curd 4-5 days ❌ Steamed sprouts 2 days ❌ Paneer (opened) 2-3 days ❌ Dishes with paneer / soya / potato same day-1 day ❌

Monsoon note on chopped veg: 3-4 days is the upper edge in humid weather. Plan menus so chopped veg is consumed by day 3; day 4 is a smell-test fallback, not a plan.

docs.title: The three non-negotiables
docs.text:
1. Cook → cool to warm (30-40 min, never hours) → lid → fridge.
  2. Reheat thoroughly — piping hot. Only the portion being eaten.
  3. Smell/look test beats any table. When in doubt, throw it out.

docs.title: How the System Works
docs.text: Solo vegetarian · Hyderabad monsoon · work 12 PM-9 PM · one morning cook covers lunch + dinner.

docs.title: The two-layer model
docs.text:
Layer 1 — Weekend prep (one session, 1.5-2 hrs): make components, not meals — masala base, plain-boiled legumes and dal, boiled potatoes, chopped veggie boxes, curd, sprouts, batch rotis/theplas, sambar-style dal. Components are flexible: the same boiled chana becomes chole one day and chaat another.

Layer 2 — Weekday cooking (25-45 min each morning): dishes that consume the components. Heat base + add boiled legumes = gravy in 10 minutes. Fresh tadka on pre-boiled dal. Rice always cooked fresh (it's the worst prep candidate and the easiest fresh cook).

Every recipe on this site states which layer it belongs to (Type: weekend-prep or weekday-dish) and which components it uses.

docs.title: The three non-negotiables
docs.text:
1. Cook → cool to warm (30-40 min, never hours) → lid → fridge. Morning food never waits on the counter till evening.
  2. Reheat thoroughly — piping hot, not lukewarm. Reheat only the portion you're eating.
  3. Smell/look test beats any table. Monsoon humidity shortens everything by a day if the fridge is opened often. When in doubt, throw it out.

docs.title: Daily flow
docs.text:
• Morning (25-45 min): cook the day's dish → cool while getting ready → fridge before work
  • Lunch: reheat portion 1
  • Dinner: reheat portion 2 in a kadai on low-medium with a splash of water till piping hot · 🔲 microwave: 2-3 min in the same glass box
  • Night (5 min): move tomorrow's freezer portion to fridge · scrape scraps to bin · rinse utensils · boiling water down the sink every other night

docs.title: Weekend prep sequence (everything overlaps)
docs.text:
1. Put legume round 1 in the pressure cooker
  2. Start masala base in the kadai (stir between tasks)
  3. Swap cooker batches as whistles finish (legume 2 → dal → potatoes)
  4. Chop & box veggies while the base cooks down; knead/cook theplas if the week's plan uses them
  5. Cool everything with lids ajar → label → fridge/freezer
  6. Night: set curd, soak legumes/sprouts for the coming days

docs.title: Hygiene
docs.text: Instahelp every 2-3 days for utensils + mopping. Pre-rinse everything so nothing cakes on; flex the schedule by nose in peak humidity.

docs.title: Weekly Variations
docs.text: Each variation = one weekend prep session (~1.5-2 hrs) + a 6-day menu, sized for ~10-11 home meals a week. Office-lunch day and outside meals slot anywhere — shift the menu down a day; the freezer absorbs the surplus. Same-day dishes (pongal, pav bhaji, matar paneer, soya bhurji) never appear as multi-day leftovers.

docs.title: Pick your week
docs.text: Variation Character Protein Effort Freezer dependency 1 North Indian core Good Medium Low 2 South Indian lean Moderate Low Medium 3 Light / gym (sprouts) Good Low-medium Low 4 High-protein North (soya, paneer) Strong Medium Low-medium 5 Light South/North mixed Moderate-good Low Medium

docs.title: Variation 1 — North Indian Core
docs.text:
Weekend prep (≈ 1 hr 45 min, parallelised):

  • Pressure cooker rounds: rajma (soaked overnight) → chana → toor dal → potatoes
  • Kadai: onion-tomato masala base
  • Chop & box: lauki, gobi, onions
  • Set curd at night; Sunday shop: veggies + 1 pack idli batter
Day Meal (lunch = dinner) Morning time Mon Dal tadka + jeera aloo + rice 25 min Tue Rajma chawal + curd 20 min Wed Idli + sambar-style dal 30 min Thu Chole + rice or batch rotis 25 min Fri Lauki sabzi (base + lauki box) + rotis + dal (freezer) 35 min Sat Gobi-aloo + leftover rotis / or meal out 25 min

docs.title: Variation 2 — South Indian Lean Week
docs.text:
Weekend prep (≈ 1.5 hrs):

  • Big batch sambar-style dal (double: 2 cups toor, with lauki + frozen veg)
  • Boil: whole moong + potatoes
  • Masala base (or use freezer stock from last week)
  • Chop & box: beans/gawar, onions
  • Buy 2 packs idli batter (pack 2 from Wednesday); set curd
Day Meal Morning time Mon Idli + sambar-style dal 15 min Tue Whole moong curry (base + boiled moong) + rice 20 min Wed Veg daliya khichdi (one pot) 25 min Thu Idli + sambar-style dal (freezer portion) 15 min Fri Beans/gawar sabzi + rice + dal 35 min Sat Dosa + aloo masala (boiled potatoes!) — the weekend treat 45 min

docs.title: Variation 3 — Light / Gym Week
docs.text:
Weekend prep (≈ 1.5 hrs):

  • Soak moong/moth for sprouts (ready Tue) + second sprout batch Wednesday (ready Fri)
  • Boil: chana + moong dal + potatoes
  • Masala base, curd
  • Chop & box: kaddu or lauki, onions
Day Meal Morning time Mon Moong dal khichdi + curd (one pot) 20 min Tue Steamed sprout salad bowl + 2 theplas (batch-made today for the week) 40 min Wed Chana chaat (boiled chana + onion + tomato + potato + curd) + thepla 15 min Thu Kaddu/lauki sabzi + dal + rice 30 min Fri Sprout bowl (batch 2) + thepla + curd 10 min Sat Aloo-mangodi curry + rice 25 min

docs.title: Variation 4 — High-Protein North Indian Week
docs.text:
Weekend prep (≈ 2 hrs, parallelised):

  • Saturday night: soak rajma + chana separately
  • Pressure cooker rounds: rajma (5-6 whistles + low 10 min) → chana (5-6) → moong/toor dal (3-4) → potatoes (3)
  • Kadai: onion-tomato masala base
  • Chop & box: gobi, beans/gawar, lauki, onions
  • Batch theplas (6-7): knead while cooker rounds run, cook on tawa alongside masala base
  • Freezer: extra rajma/chana/dal/base in single-meal portions
  • Paneer: buy fresh Friday/Saturday for the matar paneer day — it won't last from the weekend shop
  • Set curd at night
  • Sprout timeline: Sunday night soak ½ cup moong/moth → Monday morning drain into covered colander → rinse/drain Mon evening + Tue morning → Tuesday steam 8-10 min before eating. (If prepping Saturday, start the soak Saturday night.)
Day Meal Morning time Mon Rajma chawal + curd 20 min Tue Steamed sprout bowl + 2-3 theplas + curd 15 min Wed Soya bhurji + rotis 25 min Thu Lauki kootu + rice 25 min Fri Kadai vegetable + rotis 30 min Sat Matar paneer + rotis/rice 20 min

Protein balance: strong — rajma, steamed sprouts, soya, dal-based kootu, paneer, peas. Freezer-dependent days: Thu (frozen boiled dal); optionally Mon (frozen rajma from an earlier week).

docs.title: Variation 5 — Light South/North Mixed Week
docs.text:
Weekend prep (≈ 2 hrs, parallelised):

  • Saturday night: soak chole/chana + moth/moong separately
  • Pressure cooker rounds: chole/chana (5-6 whistles) → whole moong/moth (2-3) → toor/moong dal (3-4) → potatoes (3)
  • Kadai: onion-tomato masala base
  • Chop & box: lauki, gobi, gawar/beans, onions
  • Make + portion sambar-style dal: 2 fridge, 2 freezer
  • Batch theplas (6-7): knead while cooker rounds run, cook on tawa alongside masala base
  • Freezer: extra chana/moong/dal/base
  • Set curd at night
  • Sprout timeline: same as Variation 4
Day Meal Morning time Mon Idli + sambar-style dal 15 min Tue Steamed sprout bowl + theplas + curd 15 min Wed Dalia pulao 25 min Thu Chole + rice or rotis 25 min Fri Ven/Kara pongal 25 min Sat Pav bhaji + rotis/dosa/fresh pav 25 min

Protein balance: moderate-good — chole, sprouts, dalia with frozen veg, sambar dal, moong dal in pongal; lighter than Variation 4. Freezer-dependent days: Mon (frozen sambar-dal), Thu (frozen chole), Fri (frozen boiled moong dal).

Bhindi/brinjal days: swap into any variation's Fri/Sat slot, cooked fresh, eaten same day.

docs.title: Aloo Pyaaz Bhindi Sabji
docs.text:
Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: Same day to 1 day; refrigerate the dinner portion after cooling 30-40 min · Freezer-safe: No, because the dish contains potato Active time: 30-35 min (morning) · Makes: 2 meals for 1 person Uses prepped components: none Source: own

Style: dry, lightly masala-coated aloo-pyaaz bhindi. Pairs with 3 rotis per meal.

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Bhindi 200-230 g after trimming, from about 250 g untrimmed Potato 1 medium, about 140-160 g, cut into 1 cm cubes Onion 1 medium, about 90-100 g, thinly sliced Green chilli 1-2, finely chopped Oil 1½ tbsp Jeera ½ tsp Hing 1 pinch Haldi ¼ tsp Dhania powder 1 tsp Red chilli powder ¼ tsp, optional Amchur ½ tsp Garam masala ¼ tsp, optional Salt ¾ tsp, adjust to taste Water 2 tbsp, for partially cooking the potato

docs.title: Process
docs.text:
1. Make sure the bhindi is completely dry. Cut it fresh into approximately 2-2½ cm pieces. Cut the potato into 1 cm cubes, thinly slice the onion, and finely chop the green chilli.
  2. Heat 1½ tbsp oil in a kadai over medium heat. Add ½ tsp jeera and let it crackle for 15-20 seconds, then add 1 pinch hing.
  3. Add the potato, ¼ tsp haldi, and ¼ tsp salt. Sauté over medium heat for 2 minutes.
  4. Add 2 tbsp water around the sides of the kadai. Cover and cook over low heat for 5-6 minutes, stirring once halfway through, until the potato is about half to two-thirds cooked.
  5. Remove the lid and cook over medium heat for 1-2 minutes, until all visible moisture has evaporated.
  6. Add the onion and green chilli. Cook over medium heat for 3-4 minutes, until the onion softens slightly.
  7. Add the chopped bhindi and mix gently. Cook uncovered over medium heat for 5-6 minutes, stirring only once or twice so the bhindi does not break or become sticky.
  8. Add 1 tsp dhania powder, optional ¼ tsp red chilli powder, and the remaining ½ tsp salt. Mix gently.
  9. Continue cooking uncovered over medium-low heat for 7-9 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the bhindi is cooked and the potato is tender.
  10. Add ½ tsp amchur and optional ¼ tsp garam masala. Mix gently and cook for another 1-2 minutes.
  11. Switch off the heat and leave the sabji uncovered for 3-5 minutes so excess steam escapes before portioning.

docs.title: Portioning and Storage
docs.text:
• Divide the sabji into 2 equal portions. Serve one portion for lunch with 3 rotis.
  • Transfer the dinner portion to a separate clean container so it is not repeatedly handled.
  • Cool the dinner portion to warm within 30-40 minutes, then cover and refrigerate immediately.
  • Consume the refrigerated portion at dinner the same day; the maximum fridge life is 1 day.
  • Reheat only the dinner portion in a kadai over low-medium heat for 4-5 minutes, stirring occasionally, until piping hot throughout.
  • If the sabji looks dry while reheating, add 1 tsp oil. Add only 1 tsp water around the kadai edge if absolutely necessary.
  • Do not refrigerate or reheat the portion again. Do not freeze because the dish contains potato.

docs.title: Aloo-Pyaaz-Tamatar Jhol Wali Sabji
docs.text:
Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: 1 day; refrigerate the dinner portion after cooling 30-40 min · Freezer-safe: No, because potato texture deteriorates after freezing Active time: 20-25 min (morning) · Makes: 2 meals for 1 person Uses prepped components: none Source: own

Style: thin, spoonable jhol gravy — the runny cousin of the semi-dry Aloo Pyaaz Tamatar Sabzi. Pairs with 3 rotis per meal.

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Potato 3 medium, about 300-350 g Onion 1 medium, finely chopped Tomato 2 medium, finely chopped Garlic 5-6 cloves, finely chopped/crushed Ginger ½-inch piece, grated Green chilli 1, finely chopped/slit Oil 1½ tbsp Jeera ½ tsp Hing 1 pinch Haldi ½ tsp Red chilli powder ¼-½ tsp Dhania powder 1½ tsp Jeera powder ½ tsp Salt 1 tsp, or to taste Garam masala ¼ tsp Amchur ¼ tsp, optional Water 2½ cups Kasuri methi ½ tsp, optional Fresh coriander 1 tbsp, optional

docs.title: Process — Pressure Cooker
docs.text:
1. Peel the potatoes and cut them into approximately 1-inch pieces. Keep the pieces medium-sized so they do not completely dissolve during pressure cooking.
  2. Heat 1½ tbsp oil in the pressure cooker on medium flame.
  3. Add ½ tsp jeera. Once it crackles, add the hing.
  4. Add the chopped onion and cook for 4-5 minutes, until soft and lightly golden.
  5. Add garlic, ginger and green chilli. Cook for 1 minute, until the raw garlic smell reduces.
  6. Add the tomatoes, ½ tsp salt, haldi, red chilli powder, dhania powder and jeera powder.
  7. Cook for 5-6 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the tomatoes become soft and the masala begins leaving oil at the edges.
  8. Add the chopped potatoes and cook them with the masala for 2 minutes.
  9. Add 2½ cups water and the remaining salt. Mix well and scrape any masala stuck to the bottom.
  10. Close the pressure cooker and cook on medium flame for 2 whistles.
  11. Turn off the flame and allow the pressure to release naturally for approximately 8-10 minutes.
  12. Open the cooker and lightly mash 3-4 potato pieces against the side of the cooker. This will slightly thicken the gravy while keeping it sufficiently liquid.
  13. Add garam masala, optional amchur and crushed kasuri methi.
  14. Simmer uncovered for 3-5 minutes. Add another ¼ cup hot water if the gravy has become thicker than desired.
  15. Taste and adjust the salt. Add coriander, if using.

docs.title: Portioning and Storage
docs.text:
• Divide the sabji into 2 equal portions immediately after cooking.
  • Keep the lunch portion covered for serving.
  • Let the dinner portion cool for 30-40 minutes, then transfer it to a covered container and refrigerate.
  • At dinner, reheat only that portion in a small kadai on low-medium flame with 2-3 tbsp water, stirring occasionally, until piping hot.

docs.title: Aloo Pyaaz Tamatar Sabzi
docs.text:
Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: same day best; dinner portion safe if cooled and refrigerated within 30-40 min · Freezer-safe: No, because potato texture spoils Active time: 20-25 min (morning) · Makes: 2 meals for 1 person Uses prepped components: none Source: own Kitchen-tested: ✅ Yes

Style: semi-dry sabzi, not watery gravy. Pairs with 6 rotis + plain moong dal.

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Potato 2 medium, ~280-300 g Onion 1 medium, finely chopped or thin sliced Tomato 1 medium, finely chopped Green chilli 1, slit/chopped optional Oil 1.5 tbsp Jeera ¾ tsp Hing 1 pinch optional Haldi ¼ tsp Red chilli powder ½ tsp Dhania powder 1 tsp Salt ¾ tsp, adjust Garam masala ¼ tsp Amchur ¼ tsp optional Water 3-5 tbsp

docs.title: Process
docs.text:
1. Peel potatoes and cut into small cubes, around 1-1.5 cm. Smaller cubes cook faster.
  2. Heat 1.5 tbsp oil in kadai.
  3. Add jeera + hing. Let jeera crackle.
  4. Add onion + green chilli + a pinch of salt. Cook on medium flame for 4-5 min until onion turns light golden.
  5. Add tomato + haldi + red chilli + dhania powder + salt.
  6. Cook 4-5 min until tomato becomes soft and oil starts separating slightly.
  7. Add potato cubes. Mix well for 1-2 min so masala coats the potatoes.
  8. Add 3-5 tbsp water, cover, and cook on low-medium flame for 10-12 min.
  9. Open and check. If potatoes are not soft, sprinkle 1-2 tbsp more water and cook covered for another 3-5 min.
  10. Once potatoes are cooked, cook uncovered for 2-3 min to make it semi-dry.
  11. Add garam masala + amchur. Mix and switch off.

docs.title: Storage for dinner
docs.text:
• Remove dinner portion before eating lunch.
  • Cool 30-40 min max, then refrigerate in a covered box.
  • Reheat in kadai with 1-2 tbsp water, covered for 3-4 min, then dry it slightly.

docs.title: Batch Rotis / Theplas
docs.text:
Type: weekend-prep Fridge life: rotis 2-3 days · theplas 3-4 days · Freezer-safe: theplas yes (1 week); rotis no Active time: 30-40 min per batch · Makes: 6 rotis or 6-7 theplas Uses prepped components: none Source: own, validated

Never store dough — it greys and sours in a day. Store cooked rotis instead.

Rotis (2-day batch): for 6 rotis, use 210-240 g atta, ½ tsp salt optional, 1 tsp oil optional, and about 130-150 ml water. Knead soft, rest 10-15 min, roll and cook 6 rotis. Smear light ghee only after cooking if desired. Cool on a plate/wire rack for 5-8 min so steam escapes, then stack in a cloth-lined airtight box and fridge. Revive on a hot tawa 30 sec/side — or 🔲 microwave 20 sec wrapped in a slightly damp cloth.

Theplas (3-4 day batch, better keeper): for 6-7 theplas, knead 180 g atta + 45 g besan, ¼ cup curd, 1 tbsp oil, ½ tsp salt, ½ tsp ajwain, ¼ tsp haldi, ½ tsp chilli powder, and optional grated lauki. Add water only as needed to make a firm but rollable dough. Roll thin, cook on medium flame with a little oil on both sides till brown spots appear. Cool fully, then stack in an airtight box with paper/cloth between layers. These stay soft 3-4 days in the fridge — the best roti-family option for meal prep. Fridge is the default in monsoon; their room-temp tolerance is for office-day tiffins, not counter storage.

Lazy-day rule: if rotis feel like effort, default to rice. Rice is always fresh (1 bowl = ¾ cup rice + 1.5 cups water, pressure cook 2 whistles, zero attention while you cook the sabzi).

docs.title: Boiled Potatoes
docs.text:
Type: weekend-prep Fridge life: 3-4 days · Freezer-safe: no (never freeze) Active time: 1 cooker cycle, zero attention · Makes: 5-6 medium potatoes Uses prepped components: none Source: own, validated

Batch: 5-6 medium potatoes | Time: 1 pressure-cooker cycle (3 whistles), zero attention

  1. Wash potatoes well. Keep skin ON.
  2. Add 1 cup water to the pressure cooker. Place potatoes directly in the cooker or on a separator/stand if available.
  3. Pressure cook for 3 whistles on medium flame. For very large potatoes, use 4 whistles.
  4. Let pressure release naturally.
  5. Check doneness with a knife: it should slide in easily, but potatoes should not be collapsing.
  6. Remove potatoes from the cooker water and cool fully on a plate/colander so surface moisture dries.
  7. Store unpeeled in a steel dabba. Fridge 3-4 days. Never freeze (texture turns grainy).

Becomes: jeera aloo (5 min), aloo-mangodi, aloo added to poha, quick bhaji mash with masala base, aloo raita with your curd.

docs.title: Chopped Veggie Boxes
docs.text:
Type: weekend-prep Fridge life: 3-4 days (plan to consume by day 3) · Freezer-safe: no Active time: 20-30 min chopping · Makes: 1 week of hardy veg Uses prepped components: none Source: own, validated

On prep day: wash veggies before chopping, drain well, then dry completely on a cloth (moisture = monsoon spoilage). Chop only after the surface is dry. Store in airtight boxes with a paper towel/tissue at the bottom to absorb condensation. Keep boxes on the cold fridge shelf, not in the door.

Veggie Chop ahead? Notes Lauki, toru, kaddu ✅ 3-4 days Peel, chop, dry again if watery, then box with tissue Gobi ✅ 3-4 days Cut florets, rinse/check well, dry very thoroughly before boxing Gawar fali, beans ✅ 3-4 days String + chop; keep very dry Onion ✅ 2-3 days Peel and chop dry; do not wash after chopping; separate sealed box (smell) Tomato ❌ Goes watery — chop fresh (2 min) Bhindi ❌ The exception Wash a day ahead, dry FULLY on cloth, store whole, chop fresh (5 min on dry bhindi) Brinjal ❌ Browns when cut; cook fresh only

Frozen peas/carrot/beans need nothing — they're already prepped. Your emergency arsenal.

docs.title: Dalia Pulao
docs.text: Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: 2 days · Freezer-safe: yes (2 weeks) Morning active time: 25 min · Serves: 1 person, lunch + dinner Uses prepped components: frozen veg mix (pantry); no weekend prep needed Source: My Tasty Curry Dalia Pulao / one-pot Indian vegetarian dinner, adapted for pressure cooker

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Dalia / broken wheat 1 cup Frozen peas-carrot-beans mix 1½ cups Onion 1 small, chopped Tomato 1 small, chopped, optional Oil or ghee 2 tsp Jeera 1 tsp Bay leaf 1, optional Green chilli 1, slit Ginger ½ inch, grated Haldi ¼ tsp Red chilli powder ½ tsp Garam masala ½ tsp Salt ¾ tsp or to taste Water 2½ cups Lemon juice 1 tsp Coriander 1 tbsp, optional Boiled potato 1 small, cubed, optional fridge-only add

docs.title: Method
docs.text:
1. Dry roast dalia in the pressure cooker base for 3-4 min on low-medium flame till lightly nutty. Remove to a plate.
  2. Heat oil or ghee in the same cooker.
  3. Add jeera and bay leaf. Let splutter.
  4. Add onion, green chilli, and ginger. Sauté 2-3 min.
  5. Add tomato if using. Cook 2 min till slightly soft.
  6. Add frozen peas-carrot-beans mix, haldi, red chilli powder, garam masala, and salt. Sauté 2 min.
  7. Add roasted dalia and mix well.
  8. Add water. Mix and pressure cook for 2 whistles on medium flame.
  9. Let pressure release naturally for 5 min, then open.
  10. Fluff gently. Finish with lemon juice and coriander.
  11. If using boiled potato, mix it after opening and simmer 2 min; then treat the dish as fridge-only, not freezer-safe.

docs.title: Portion & Store
docs.text:
• Split into 2 portions: lunch + dinner.
  • Cool 30-40 min, then refrigerate.
  • Fridge life: 2 days max in monsoon.
  • Freezer-safe only when made without potato; freeze single-meal portions up to 2 weeks.
  • Reheat in kadai with 2-3 tbsp water.

docs.title: What it becomes / variations
docs.text: • Add boiled moong dal or paneer cubes for a higher-protein version; keep potato out if freezing.

docs.title: Fresh Coconut, Peanut and Roasted Chana Chutney
docs.text:
Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: Same day; keep refrigerated and consume the dinner portion by evening · Freezer-safe: No, because fresh coconut chutney loses its texture and fresh flavour after freezing Active time: 10-12 min (morning) · Makes: 2 side portions for 1 person Uses prepped components: none Source: own

Style: thick, coconut-forward South Indian chutney for about 4 idlis per meal.

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Fresh mature coconut flesh 70-80 g, about ¾ cup chopped Roasted chana dal / dalia / putani, without peel 1 tbsp Peanuts 2 tsp Green chilli 1 small, adjust to taste Ginger ½-inch piece Garlic 1 small clove Curry leaves 4-5 leaves Salt ⅓ tsp, adjust to taste Water ¼-⅓ cup, as needed Lemon juice ½ tsp, optional

docs.title: Optional Tempering
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Oil 1 tsp Mustard seeds ¼ tsp Urad dal ¼ tsp, optional Curry leaves 4-5 leaves Hing 1 small pinch

docs.title: Process
docs.text:
1. Remove approximately 70-80 g coconut flesh and cut it into small pieces.
  2. Dry-roast 2 tsp peanuts over low heat for 3-4 minutes, until lightly coloured and aromatic. Let them cool completely.
  3. Add the coconut, roasted peanuts, 1 tbsp roasted chana dal, 1 small green chilli, ½-inch ginger, 1 small garlic clove, 4-5 curry leaves, and ⅓ tsp salt to the mixer jar.
  4. Add only ¼ cup water initially.
  5. Grind using short pulses, scraping down the sides as required, until reasonably smooth.
  6. Check the consistency. Add more water only 1 tbsp at a time. Keep the total water between approximately ¼ and ⅓ cup.
  7. Taste and adjust the salt. Mix in ½ tsp lemon juice, if using.
  8. For the optional tempering, heat 1 tsp oil over medium heat.
  9. Add ¼ tsp mustard seeds. Once they splutter, add ¼ tsp urad dal, 4-5 curry leaves, and a small pinch of hing.
  10. Cook for 20-30 seconds, switch off the heat, and pour the tempering over the chutney.

docs.title: Portioning and Storage
docs.text:
• This quantity makes approximately ¾ cup thick chutney, sufficient for two idli meals.
  • Divide the chutney into 2 separate portions after preparation.
  • Keep both portions refrigerated if lunch is not being eaten immediately.
  • Do not leave the chutney at room temperature for more than 30-40 minutes before covering and refrigerating it.
  • Remove only the portion being served; keep the dinner portion refrigerated.
  • Consume the refrigerated dinner portion by the same evening.
  • Do not reheat coconut chutney. Let the required portion sit outside for 10-15 minutes before serving if you do not want it fridge-cold.
  • Store unused coconut pieces separately in a clean, dry, airtight container. Do not store them submerged in coconut water.
  • Do not freeze the chutney.

docs.title: Fresh Rotis — 1 person, lunch + dinner
docs.text:
Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: cooked rotis 2-3 days, but best same day · Freezer-safe: No Active time: 25-35 min (morning) · Makes: 6 rotis = 3 lunch + 3 dinner Uses prepped components: none Source: own Kitchen-tested: ✅ Yes

Storage rule: do not store dough; store cooked rotis only, as the plan notes for batch rotis.

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Atta 220 g Water 140-150 ml, add gradually Salt ¼ tsp optional Oil or ghee in dough 1 tsp optional Ghee for smear ½-1 tsp total optional

docs.title: Process
docs.text:
1. Add 220 g atta + salt in a bowl/parat.
  2. Add 140 ml water gradually and mix. Add 1-2 tsp extra only if dry.
  3. Knead 5-6 min until smooth-medium soft, not sticky.
  4. Apply ½ tsp oil/ghee on top, cover, rest 10-15 min.
  5. Divide into 6 equal balls.
  6. Roll each roti with dry atta.

  7. Heat tawa well. Cook roti:

    • Side 1: 20-30 sec, light spots
    • Side 2: 30-40 sec
    • Flip again and puff/press gently

  8. Lightly smear ghee if you want softer rotis.

docs.title: Storage for lunch + dinner
docs.text:
• Lunch 3 rotis: keep wrapped in clean cotton cloth inside a casserole/covered box.
  • Dinner 3 rotis: cool fully for 20-30 min, wrap in cloth/foil, keep in airtight box, refrigerate.
  • Reheat dinner rotis on hot tawa 20-30 sec per side. Sprinkle a few drops of water only if dry.

docs.title: Gawar Aloo Lipta Masala Sabji
docs.text:
Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: Same day to 1 day; refrigerate the dinner portion after cooling 30-40 min · Freezer-safe: No, because the dish contains potato Active time: 30-35 min (morning) · Makes: 2 meals for 1 person Uses prepped components: none Source: own

Style: semi-dry gawar and potato sabji with thick masala coating. Pairs with 3 rotis per meal.

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Gawar fali 300-350 g before trimming, cut into 2 cm pieces Potato 1 medium, about 130-150 g Onion 1 medium, about 90-100 g, finely chopped Tomato 1 large, about 120-140 g, finely chopped Garlic 4 cloves, finely chopped or crushed Green chilli 1, finely chopped Oil 1½ tbsp Jeera ½ tsp Ajwain ¼ tsp Hing 1 generous pinch Haldi ¼ tsp Red chilli powder ½ tsp, adjust to taste Dhania powder 1½ tsp Jeera powder ½ tsp Salt ¾ tsp, or to taste Water ⅓ cup, about 80 ml Amchur ½ tsp Garam masala ¼ tsp Kasuri methi ½ tsp, optional

docs.title: Process
docs.text:
1. Peel the potato and cut it into small 1-1.5 cm cubes. Keep the pieces smaller than the gawar so the potato cooks quickly.
  2. Heat 1½ tbsp oil in a kadai over medium heat.
  3. Add ½ tsp jeera and ¼ tsp ajwain. Once they crackle, add 1 generous pinch hing.
  4. Add the chopped onion and cook over medium heat for 4-5 minutes, until soft and lightly golden.
  5. Add the garlic and green chilli. Sauté for 1 minute.
  6. Add the chopped tomato, ¼ tsp haldi, ½ tsp red chilli powder, 1½ tsp dhania powder, ½ tsp jeera powder, and ½ tsp salt.
  7. Cook over medium heat for 5-6 minutes, stirring and mashing the tomato, until it becomes pulpy and the masala begins leaving oil at the edges.
  8. Add the potato cubes. Mix and sauté with the masala over medium heat for 3 minutes.
  9. Add the chopped gawar and the remaining salt. Mix thoroughly and sauté uncovered for 4-5 minutes.
  10. Add ⅓ cup water and scrape any masala stuck to the bottom of the kadai.
  11. Cover and cook over low-medium heat for 16-20 minutes. Stir every 4-5 minutes.
  12. If the mixture becomes completely dry before the potato and gawar are tender, add 1-2 tbsp hot water at a time.
  13. Check the potato with a spoon and taste one thicker gawar piece. Both should be fully tender.
  14. Remove the lid and cook over medium heat for 4-6 minutes, stirring regularly. Lightly press 2-3 potato cubes against the side of the kadai and mix them back into the sabji. This thickens the masala and gives the desired lipta texture.
  15. Add ½ tsp amchur, ¼ tsp garam masala, and optional ½ tsp crushed kasuri methi.
  16. Cook uncovered for another 1-2 minutes, stirring gently, until the thick masala coats the gawar and potato.
  17. Taste and adjust the salt or amchur. The finished sabji should be moist and masala-coated, but should not have loose gravy.

docs.title: Portioning and Storage
docs.text:
• Divide the sabji into 2 equal portions, one each for lunch and dinner.
  • Each portion pairs with 3 rotis.
  • Remove the dinner portion from the hot kadai and allow it to cool for 30-40 minutes.
  • Transfer it to a covered container and refrigerate immediately.
  • Because the dish contains potato, consume it by dinner the same day or within 1 day maximum.
  • At dinner, reheat only the portion being eaten in a kadai with 1-2 tbsp water.
  • Cover and reheat over low-medium heat for 4-5 minutes, stirring once or twice, until piping hot throughout.
  • Do not freeze this sabji.

docs.title: Gawar Dal Sabji
docs.text:
Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: 2-3 days; refrigerate the dinner portion after cooling 30-40 min · Freezer-safe: No, because cooked gawar loses texture after freezing Active time: 35-40 min (morning) · Makes: 2 meals for 1 person Uses prepped components: none Source: own

Style: semi-dry gawar sabji with lightly spiced dal. Pairs with 3 rotis per meal.

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Gawar fali 350-400 g before trimming Yellow moong dal ⅓ cup, about 65-70 g Water for soaking Enough to cover the dal Onion 1 small, about 70-80 g, finely chopped Garlic 4 cloves, finely chopped Green chilli 1-2, finely chopped Oil 1½ tbsp Jeera ½ tsp Hing 1 generous pinch Haldi ¼ tsp Red chilli powder ½ tsp, adjust to taste Dhania powder 1 tsp Salt ¾ tsp, or to taste Water for cooking ½ cup, about 120 ml Amchur ½ tsp Garam masala ¼ tsp, optional

docs.title: Process
docs.text:
1. Rinse ⅓ cup yellow moong dal until the water runs mostly clear. Soak it in enough water for 20-30 minutes, then drain.
  2. Wash the gawar thoroughly. Remove the stalk ends and pull away any tough strings from the sides.
  3. Chop the gawar into approximately 2 cm pieces.
  4. Heat 1½ tbsp oil in a kadai over medium heat.
  5. Add ½ tsp jeera. Once it crackles, add 1 generous pinch hing.
  6. Add the chopped onion and cook over medium heat for 4-5 minutes, until soft and lightly golden.
  7. Add the chopped garlic and green chilli. Sauté for 1 minute.
  8. Reduce the heat to low. Add ¼ tsp haldi, ½ tsp red chilli powder, and 1 tsp dhania powder. Stir for 10-15 seconds.
  9. Immediately add the chopped gawar and ¾ tsp salt. Mix well and sauté over medium heat for 4-5 minutes.
  10. Add the drained moong dal and mix gently with the gawar.
  11. Add ½ cup water. Cover and cook over low-medium heat for 18-22 minutes.
  12. Open and stir gently every 5 minutes. Do not stir aggressively, otherwise the moong dal may turn mushy.
  13. If the mixture becomes completely dry before the gawar and dal are cooked, add 1-2 tbsp hot water at a time.
  14. Check the gawar and dal. The gawar should be tender, and the dal should be cooked through but still retain some shape.
  15. Remove the lid and cook over medium heat for 3-5 minutes, stirring gently, until excess moisture evaporates and the sabji becomes semi-dry.
  16. Add ½ tsp amchur and optional ¼ tsp garam masala.
  17. Mix gently and cook for another 1 minute. Taste and adjust the salt or amchur.

docs.title: Chana Dal Variation
docs.text:
• Replace yellow moong dal with ⅓ cup chana dal.
  • Soak the chana dal for 1½-2 hours.
  • Pressure cook the soaked and drained chana dal with ¾ cup water and a pinch of haldi for 1 whistle on medium heat.
  • Allow the pressure to release naturally for 5 minutes, then release any remaining pressure.
  • The dal should be about 80% cooked and still hold its shape.
  • Add the partially cooked chana dal to the kadai after sautéing the gawar.
  • Add only ¼ cup of the chana-dal cooking water.
  • Cover and cook the gawar and chana dal together over low-medium heat for 15-18 minutes.
  • Continue with the amchur and garam masala finishing steps.

docs.title: Portioning and Storage
docs.text:
• Divide the sabji into 2 equal portions, one each for lunch and dinner.
  • Each portion pairs with 3 rotis.
  • Remove the dinner portion from the hot kadai and let it cool for 30-40 minutes.
  • Transfer it to a covered container and refrigerate immediately.
  • At dinner, reheat only the portion being eaten in a kadai with 1-2 tbsp water.
  • Cover and reheat over low-medium heat for 4-5 minutes, stirring once or twice, until piping hot throughout.
  • Do not freeze this sabji.

docs.title: Gawar Mangodi Sabji
docs.text:
Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: 2-3 days; refrigerate the dinner portion after cooling 30-40 min · Freezer-safe: No, because cooked gawar loses texture and releases water after thawing Active time: 35-40 min (morning) · Makes: 2 meals for 1 person Uses prepped components: none Source: own

Style: semi-dry gawar sabji with tender moong dal mangodi and light onion-tomato masala. Pairs with 3 rotis per meal.

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Gawar fali 300-350 g before trimming Moong dal mangodi ⅓ cup, about 45-50 g Onion 1 small, about 70-80 g, finely chopped Tomato 1 medium, about 90-100 g, finely chopped Garlic 4 cloves, finely chopped or crushed Green chilli 1-2, finely chopped Oil 2 tbsp, divided Jeera ½ tsp Hing 1 generous pinch Haldi ¼ tsp Red chilli powder ½ tsp, adjust to taste Dhania powder 1½ tsp Jeera powder ½ tsp Salt ¾ tsp, or to taste Water 1 cup, about 240 ml Amchur ½ tsp Garam masala ¼ tsp, optional

docs.title: Process
docs.text:
1. Wash the gawar thoroughly. Remove the stalk ends and pull away any tough strings from the sides.
  2. Chop the gawar into approximately 2 cm pieces.
  3. Heat 1 tsp oil in a kadai over low-medium heat.
  4. Add ⅓ cup mangodi and roast for 3-4 minutes, stirring continuously, until lightly golden and aromatic. Do not let it turn dark brown.
  5. Remove the roasted mangodi to a plate and keep it aside.
  6. Add the remaining oil to the same kadai and heat over medium heat.
  7. Add ½ tsp jeera. Once it crackles, add 1 generous pinch hing.
  8. Add the chopped onion and cook over medium heat for 4-5 minutes, until soft and lightly golden.
  9. Add the garlic and green chilli. Sauté for 1 minute.
  10. Add the chopped tomato, ¼ tsp haldi, ½ tsp red chilli powder, 1½ tsp dhania powder, ½ tsp jeera powder, and ½ tsp salt.
  11. Cook over medium heat for 5-6 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the tomato becomes soft and pulpy.
  12. Add the chopped gawar and the remaining salt. Mix and sauté over medium heat for 4-5 minutes.
  13. Add the roasted mangodi and mix gently.
  14. Add 1 cup water. Mix well and bring it to a gentle boil over medium heat.
  15. Cover the kadai and cook over low-medium heat for 20-25 minutes.
  16. Open and stir gently every 5 minutes. Avoid vigorous stirring because softened mangodi can break apart.
  17. Check the gawar and mangodi. The gawar should be tender, and the mangodi should be completely soft inside without being hard or chalky.
  18. If either remains undercooked, add 2-3 tbsp hot water, cover, and cook for another 5-7 minutes.
  19. Once cooked, remove the lid and cook over medium heat for 4-6 minutes, stirring gently, until the extra moisture evaporates and the sabji becomes semi-dry.
  20. Add ½ tsp amchur and optional ¼ tsp garam masala.
  21. Mix gently and cook for another 1 minute. Taste and adjust the salt or amchur.

docs.title: Portioning and Storage
docs.text:
• Divide the sabji into 2 equal portions, one each for lunch and dinner.
  • Each portion pairs with 3 rotis.
  • Remove the dinner portion from the hot kadai and let it cool for 30-40 minutes.
  • Transfer it to a covered container and refrigerate immediately.
  • At dinner, reheat only the portion being eaten in a kadai with 2 tbsp water.
  • Cover and reheat over low-medium heat for 5-6 minutes, stirring gently once or twice, until piping hot throughout.
  • Do not freeze this sabji.

docs.title: Gawar Pyaaz Tamatar Masala
docs.text:
Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: 2-3 days; refrigerate the dinner portion after cooling 30-40 min · Freezer-safe: No, because gawar becomes excessively soft and watery after thawing Active time: 30-35 min (morning) · Makes: 2 meals for 1 person Uses prepped components: none Source: own

Style: semi-dry, lightly tangy gawar sabji. Pairs with 3 rotis per meal.

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Gawar fali 400 g before trimming Onion 1 medium, about 100 g, finely chopped Tomatoes 2 medium, about 180-200 g, finely chopped Garlic 4 cloves, finely chopped Green chilli 1-2, finely chopped Oil 1½ tbsp Jeera ½ tsp Hing 1 pinch Haldi ¼ tsp Red chilli powder ½ tsp, adjust to taste Dhania powder 1 tsp Salt ¾ tsp, divided; adjust to taste Amchur ½ tsp Garam masala ¼ tsp, optional Water ¼ cup, about 60 ml

docs.title: Process
docs.text:
1. Wash the gawar fali thoroughly and drain it. Remove the stalk end and any tough strings running along the sides. Chop into approximately 2 cm pieces.
  2. Heat 1½ tbsp oil in a kadai over medium heat.
  3. Add ½ tsp jeera. Once it crackles, add 1 pinch hing.
  4. Add the chopped onion and approximately ¼ tsp salt. Cook over medium heat for 5-6 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the onion becomes soft and lightly golden.
  5. Add the chopped garlic and green chilli. Sauté for 1 minute until the raw garlic smell reduces.
  6. Add the chopped tomatoes, ¼ tsp haldi, ½ tsp red chilli powder, 1 tsp dhania powder, and the remaining ½ tsp salt.
  7. Cook over medium heat for 6-8 minutes, stirring and lightly mashing the tomatoes, until they become soft and the masala starts leaving oil at the edges.
  8. Add the chopped gawar. Mix thoroughly and sauté with the masala over medium heat for 3-4 minutes.
  9. Add ¼ cup water and mix. Cover the kadai and cook over low-medium heat for 15-18 minutes.
  10. Open and stir every 4-5 minutes so the sabji does not stick. Add only 1-2 tbsp extra water if the kadai becomes completely dry before the gawar is tender.
  11. Check a thicker piece of gawar. It should be completely cooked and tender but should still retain its shape.
  12. Remove the lid and cook over medium heat for 3-5 minutes, stirring regularly, until excess moisture evaporates and the sabji becomes semi-dry.
  13. Add ½ tsp amchur and ¼ tsp garam masala, if using. Mix and cook for another 1 minute. Taste and adjust the salt.

docs.title: Portioning and Storage
docs.text:
• Divide the sabji into 2 equal portions, one each for lunch and dinner.
  • Each portion is suitable with 3 rotis.
  • After cooking, remove the dinner portion from the hot kadai and allow it to cool for 30-40 minutes.
  • Transfer it to a covered container and refrigerate immediately. Do not leave it at room temperature until evening.
  • At dinner, reheat only the portion being eaten in a kadai with 1-2 tbsp water.
  • Cover and heat over low-medium heat for 4-5 minutes, stirring once or twice, until piping hot throughout.
  • Do not freeze this sabji.

docs.title: Gawar Tomato Jhol Sabji
docs.text:
Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: 2-3 days; refrigerate the dinner portion after cooling 30-40 min · Freezer-safe: No, because cooked gawar becomes excessively soft and watery after thawing Active time: 35-40 min (morning) · Makes: 2 meals for 1 person Uses prepped components: none Source: own

Style: tomato-forward, lightly spiced jhol with tender gawar. Pairs with 3 rotis per meal.

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Gawar fali 350-400 g before trimming Onion 1 small, about 70-80 g, finely chopped Tomatoes 3 medium, about 250-300 g, finely chopped Garlic 5 cloves, finely chopped or crushed Green chilli 1-2, finely chopped Oil 1½ tbsp Jeera ½ tsp Hing 1 generous pinch Haldi ¼ tsp Red chilli powder ½ tsp, adjust to taste Dhania powder 1½ tsp Jeera powder ½ tsp Salt ¾-1 tsp, or to taste Water 1¼ cups, about 300 ml Garam masala ¼ tsp, optional Jaggery or sugar ¼ tsp, optional; balances bitterness and acidity

docs.title: Process
docs.text:
1. Wash the gawar thoroughly. Remove the stalk ends and pull away any tough strings from the sides.
  2. Chop the gawar into approximately 2 cm pieces.
  3. Heat 1½ tbsp oil in a kadai over medium heat.
  4. Add ½ tsp jeera. Once it crackles, add 1 generous pinch hing.
  5. Add the chopped onion and cook over medium heat for 4-5 minutes, until soft and lightly golden.
  6. Add the garlic and green chilli. Sauté for 1 minute.
  7. Add the chopped tomatoes, ¼ tsp haldi, ½ tsp red chilli powder, 1½ tsp dhania powder, ½ tsp jeera powder, and ½ tsp salt.
  8. Cook over medium heat for 8-10 minutes, stirring and mashing the tomatoes occasionally, until they become pulpy and the masala begins leaving oil at the edges.
  9. Add the chopped gawar and the remaining salt. Mix thoroughly and sauté with the tomato masala over medium heat for 4-5 minutes.
  10. Add 1¼ cups water and mix well.
  11. Cover the kadai and cook over low-medium heat for 20-25 minutes, stirring every 5 minutes.
  12. Check one of the thicker gawar pieces. It should be completely tender and should not feel stringy or fibrous.
  13. If the gawar is still firm, add 2-3 tbsp hot water, cover, and cook for another 5 minutes.
  14. Once the gawar is cooked, lightly press a few tomato pieces against the side of the kadai to give the jhol a slightly thicker consistency.
  15. Simmer uncovered over medium heat for 4-5 minutes. The gravy should remain thin and spoonable, not completely dry.
  16. Add optional ¼ tsp garam masala and optional ¼ tsp jaggery or sugar. Mix and cook for 1 minute.
  17. Taste and adjust the salt. Add a small splash of hot water if the gravy has become thicker than desired.

docs.title: Portioning and Storage
docs.text:
• Divide the sabji into 2 equal portions, one each for lunch and dinner.
  • Each portion pairs with 3 rotis.
  • Remove the dinner portion from the hot kadai and let it cool for 30-40 minutes.
  • Transfer it to a covered container and refrigerate immediately. Do not leave it at room temperature until evening.
  • At dinner, reheat only the portion being eaten in a kadai over low-medium heat.
  • Add 1-2 tbsp water, cover, and reheat for 4-5 minutes, stirring once or twice, until piping hot throughout.
  • Do not freeze this sabji.

docs.title: Home Curd
docs.text:
Type: weekend-prep Fridge life: 4-5 days · Freezer-safe: no (never freeze) Active time: 10 min active + overnight set · Makes: 1 L batch, twice a week Uses prepped components: none Source: own, validated

  1. Boil 1 L milk, then simmer 2-3 min for thicker curd.
  2. Cool to warm (finger comfortably tolerable ≈ 40-45°C).
  3. In a small bowl, whisk 1-2 tsp fresh starter curd with 2 tbsp warm milk till smooth.
  4. Add this starter mix back to the milk and stir once.
  5. Cover and leave undisturbed for 6-8 hrs or overnight in a warm but not hot place. In humid/warm weather, check earlier if possible.
  6. Once set, fridge immediately. Use within 4-5 days. Set a fresh batch twice a week (e.g., prep day + Wednesday night — 5 min of work).

docs.title: Homestyle Paneer Pyaaz-Tamatar Masala
docs.text:
Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: Same day to 1 day; refrigerate the dinner portion after cooling 30-40 min · Freezer-safe: No, because paneer texture deteriorates after freezing Active time: 25-30 min (morning) · Makes: 2 meals for 1 person Uses prepped components: none Source: own

Style: soft paneer in a medium-thick homestyle onion-tomato masala. Pairs with 3 rotis per meal.

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Paneer 200 g, cut into about 2 cm cubes Onion 1 large, about 140-160 g, finely chopped Tomato 2 medium, about 180-200 g, finely chopped Garlic 4 cloves, finely chopped or crushed Green chilli 1, finely chopped Oil 1½ tbsp Jeera ½ tsp Haldi ¼ tsp Kashmiri red chilli powder ½ tsp Dhania powder 1 tsp Garam masala ¼ tsp Kasuri methi ½ tsp, crushed Salt ¾ tsp, adjust to taste Water ½ cup, plus 2-3 tbsp if needed

docs.title: Process
docs.text:
1. Cut 200 g paneer into approximately 2 cm cubes. If the paneer is firm, soak the cubes in warm water for 5 minutes, then drain.
  2. Heat 1½ tbsp oil in a kadai over medium heat. Add ½ tsp jeera and let it crackle for 15-20 seconds.
  3. Add the chopped onion and cook over medium heat for 6-8 minutes, stirring regularly, until soft and light golden.
  4. Add the garlic and green chilli. Cook for 45-60 seconds until the raw garlic smell reduces.
  5. Add the chopped tomatoes and ½ tsp salt. Cook over medium heat for 5-6 minutes, stirring and mashing occasionally, until the tomatoes become soft.
  6. Add ¼ tsp haldi, ½ tsp Kashmiri red chilli powder, and 1 tsp dhania powder. Cook over medium-low heat for 3-4 minutes, stirring regularly, until the masala becomes thick and the oil begins separating slightly.
  7. Add ½ cup water and mix well, scraping up any masala stuck to the kadai. Bring it to a gentle boil, then simmer over medium-low heat for 3 minutes.
  8. Add the paneer cubes and the remaining ¼ tsp salt. Mix gently so the paneer does not break.
  9. Cover and cook over low heat for 4 minutes only. Do not cook the paneer for too long, or it may become rubbery.
  10. Switch off the heat. Add ¼ tsp garam masala and ½ tsp crushed kasuri methi. Mix gently and rest covered for 5 minutes.
  11. Check the consistency. If the masala is thicker than preferred, mix in 2-3 tbsp hot water before serving.

docs.title: Portioning and Storage
docs.text:
• Divide the sabji into 2 equal portions. Serve one portion for lunch with 3 rotis.
  • Transfer the dinner portion to a separate clean container so it is not repeatedly handled.
  • Cool the dinner portion to warm within 30-40 minutes, then cover and refrigerate immediately.
  • Consume the refrigerated portion at dinner the same day; the maximum fridge life is 1 day.
  • Reheat only the dinner portion in a kadai with 1-2 tbsp water, covered over low-medium heat for 4-5 minutes, stirring gently once or twice, until piping hot throughout.
  • Do not refrigerate or reheat the portion again. Do not freeze.

docs.title: Kadai Vegetable
docs.text: Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: 2 days · Freezer-safe: no Morning active time: 30 min first time, ~22 min with jarred kadai masala · Serves: 1 person, lunch + dinner Uses prepped components: onion-tomato masala base — 150 g / 4 heaped tbsp; chopped hardy veg box — 450-500 g Source: Hebbars Kitchen Veg Kadai, adapted for prepped masala base and hardy monsoon veg

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Onion-tomato masala base 150 g / 4 heaped tbsp Chopped gobi 2 cups Chopped beans or gawar 1½ cups Frozen peas ½ cup Oil 2 tsp Jeera ½ tsp Ginger-garlic paste ½ tsp, optional Haldi ¼ tsp Salt ¾ tsp or to taste Water ½ to ¾ cup Kasuri methi ½ tsp, crushed, optional Garam masala ¼ tsp Coriander 1 tbsp, optional

docs.title: Ingredients — quick kadai masala
docs.text:
Tip: make 3x this quantity once (3 tbsp coriander seeds, 6 dry red chillies, 1½ tsp cumin), store in an airtight jar (keeps 3-4 weeks), and skip Method steps 1-2 thereafter — use 2 tsp from the jar.

Ingredient Quantity Coriander seeds 1 tbsp Dry red chilli 2 Cumin seeds ½ tsp Black pepper ¼ tsp, optional

docs.title: Method
docs.text:
1. Dry roast coriander seeds, dry red chilli, cumin seeds, and black pepper on low flame for 2-3 min till aromatic.
  2. Cool 1 min, then crush coarse in mortar-pestle or mixer pulse mode. Keep aside.
  3. Heat oil in kadai. Add jeera and ginger-garlic paste. Sauté 30 sec.
  4. Add gobi, beans or gawar, frozen peas, haldi, and salt.
  5. Sauté on medium-high for 4-5 min so vegetables get slight colour.
  6. Add ½ cup water, cover, and cook till just tender: gobi/beans 8-10 min; gawar 12-15 min.
  7. Add onion-tomato masala base and crushed kadai masala.
  8. Mix well and cook uncovered 5-7 min till masala coats the vegetables.
  9. Add kasuri methi and garam masala. Mix and switch off.
  10. Keep texture semi-dry for rotis; add a splash of water if eating with rice.

docs.title: Portion & Store
docs.text:
• Split into 2 portions: lunch + dinner.
  • Cool 30-40 min, then refrigerate the dinner portion.
  • Fridge life: 2 days max in monsoon.
  • Do not freeze finished kadai vegetable because chopped cooked veg texture becomes watery.

docs.title: What it becomes / variations
docs.text: • Use only gobi + peas for a fast version, or beans/gawar-heavy version for a drier roti sabzi.

docs.title: Lauki Besan Masala Paratha — Bread Maker Dough
docs.text:
Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: 1 day; refrigerate the dinner portion after cooling 30-40 min · Freezer-safe: No, because cooked parathas are not freezer-approved in the storage guide Active time: 45-55 min (morning) · Makes: 6-8 medium parathas, 2 meals for 1 person Uses prepped components: none Source: own

Style: soft mixed-dough lauki parathas with besan and dry masala. Serve 3-4 parathas per meal with curd or chutney.

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Lauki pieces 500-600 g, refrigerated from the previous evening Atta 250 g initially, about 2 cups Extra atta 30-80 g, as required during kneading and rolling Besan 50 g, about ½ cup Green chillies 2, roughly chopped Ginger 1-inch piece, optional Ajwain ½ tsp, lightly crushed Jeera ½ tsp, lightly crushed Dhania powder 1 tsp Haldi ½ tsp Red chilli powder ¼-½ tsp Amchur ¾ tsp Garam masala ¼ tsp Salt 1¼ tsp, adjust to taste Oil 2 tsp, for the dough Oil or ghee 2-3 tbsp total, for cooking

docs.title: Process
docs.text:
1. Check that the refrigerated lauki pieces are not slimy, sour-smelling or unusually discoloured. Taste a tiny raw piece; if it tastes distinctly bitter, discard the entire batch.
  2. Put the raw lauki pieces, green chillies and optional ginger in the mixer-grinder. Grind in 2-3 batches, using short pulses and without adding water, to make a coarse wet paste or fine mince rather than a completely liquid puree.
  3. Transfer the ground lauki to a sieve or clean cloth. Press firmly enough to remove the freely dripping liquid, but do not squeeze it completely dry. Reserve the extracted lauki liquid. The target is approximately 250-300 g moist lauki pulp, although the yield will vary.
  4. Add the ground lauki pulp, 2 tsp oil, ajwain, jeera, dhania powder, haldi, red chilli powder, amchur and garam masala to the bread-maker pan. Add 250 g atta, 50 g besan and 1¼ tsp salt. Start kneading immediately after adding the salt.
  5. Select only the bread maker's Dough or Knead mode. Do not run a complete bread-baking programme.
  6. Inspect the dough after 3-4 minutes. If it is wet and sticking heavily to the pan, add extra atta 1 tbsp at a time, waiting 45-60 seconds after each addition. If dry flour remains and the dough cannot come together, add the reserved lauki liquid 1 tsp at a time. Do not add normal water unless the reserved liquid is insufficient.
  7. Continue kneading for approximately 8-10 minutes, until the dough is soft, smooth and slightly softer than regular roti dough, but not wet or paste-like. Stop the machine after kneading; do not allow a long yeast-style proofing cycle.
  8. Remove the dough from the bread-maker pan, cover it and rest for only 10 minutes. Lauki and salt continue releasing moisture, so do not leave the dough resting for much longer.
  9. Lightly knead the rested dough by hand. If it has become sticky, knead in 1-2 tbsp atta. Divide it into 6 balls for larger parathas or 7-8 balls for medium parathas.
  10. Dust one ball lightly with atta and roll it into a circle approximately 15-17 cm wide, slightly thicker than a regular roti. Avoid excessive dry flour because it can make the paratha dry.
  11. Heat a tawa over medium-high heat. Place one rolled paratha on the hot tawa and cook the first side for 35-45 seconds, until small bubbles appear.
  12. Flip and cook for 30-40 seconds. Apply approximately ½-¾ tsp oil or ghee around the edges and over the surface. Flip again and cook over medium heat for 30-45 seconds, pressing lightly with a spatula, until both sides have golden-brown spots and the centre is fully cooked.
  13. Repeat with the remaining dough balls. Reduce the heat slightly if the tawa becomes too hot and the surface browns before the centre cooks.

docs.title: Portioning and Storage
docs.text:
• Serve 3-4 parathas for lunch with home curd.
  • Keep the dinner portion separate so it is not repeatedly handled.
  • Cool the dinner parathas to warm within 30-40 minutes, then transfer them to an airtight container and refrigerate immediately.
  • At dinner, reheat only the portion being eaten on a covered tawa over low-medium heat for 1-2 minutes per side, until piping hot throughout.
  • Serve the dinner portion with chutney.
  • Do not refrigerate or reheat the same portion again.
  • Cook the complete batch in the morning. If any raw dough remains, refrigerate it and use it within 1 day maximum; do not freeze the cooked parathas.

docs.title: Lauki Kootu
docs.text: Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: 2 days · Freezer-safe: yes (2 weeks) Morning active time: 25 min · Serves: 1 person, lunch + dinner Uses prepped components: boiled dal — 1½ cups cooked; chopped lauki/gawar box Source: Vidhya's Vegetarian Kitchen cabbage kootu, adapted to lauki/gawar and stovetop

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Lauki 400 g, peeled and chopped OR gawar 250 g, stringed and chopped Boiled moong dal or toor dal 1½ cups cooked Oil or coconut oil 2 tsp Mustard seeds ½ tsp Jeera ½ tsp Hing 1 pinch Dry red chilli 1-2 Curry leaves 6-8, optional Onion ½ small, chopped, optional Haldi ¼ tsp Salt ¾ tsp or to taste Water ½ to ¾ cup Black pepper ¼ tsp, optional Coriander 1 tbsp, optional

docs.title: Method
docs.text:
1. Heat oil in kadai or pressure cooker base.
  2. Add mustard seeds, jeera, hing, dry red chilli, and curry leaves. Let splutter.
  3. Add onion if using. Sauté 2 min.
  4. Add lauki or gawar, haldi, salt, and ½ cup water.
  5. Cover and cook till soft: lauki 10-12 min; gawar 15-18 min. Add splashes of water if drying.
  6. Add boiled dal and mix well.
  7. Simmer 5-7 min, mashing slightly for thick kootu texture.
  8. Finish with black pepper and coriander.

docs.title: Portion & Store
docs.text:
• Split into 2 portions: lunch + dinner.
  • Cool 30-40 min, then refrigerate.
  • Fridge life: 2 days max in monsoon.
  • Freezer: freeze only if made without onion garnish and cooled properly; use within 2 weeks.

docs.title: What it becomes / variations
docs.text: • Use toru instead of lauki; use gawar when you want a drier, roti-friendly kootu.

docs.title: Masala Oats — 1 person lunch
docs.text: Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: best eaten fresh; same-day fridge only if needed · Freezer-safe: No — oats + potato texture becomes bad Active time: 20-25 min (morning) · Makes: 1 filling lunch bowl Uses prepped components: none — fresh one-pot dish Source: own Kitchen-tested: ✅ Yes

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Plain oats ⅔ cup Potato 1 small / 80-100 g, small cubes Onion 1 small / 50-60 g, chopped Tomato 1 small / 60-70 g, chopped Green chilli 1, chopped Oil or ghee 1.5 tsp Jeera ½ tsp Hing 1 pinch Haldi ¼ tsp Red chilli powder ¼ tsp Dhania powder ½ tsp Garam masala 1 pinch, optional Salt ¾ tsp, adjust later Water 1¾ cups total Lemon juice ½ tsp, optional

docs.title: Pressure cooker method — recommended
docs.text:
This method uses the pressure cooker mainly to cook the potato properly. Add oats after opening so they don't become gluey.

  1. Heat 1.5 tsp oil/ghee in the pressure cooker.
  2. Add jeera + hing. Let jeera crackle.
  3. Add onion + green chilli. Sauté 3-4 min.
  4. Add potato cubes + salt + haldi. Mix well.
  5. Add ½ cup water.
  6. Close cooker. Cook for 1 whistle on medium flame.
  7. Switch off. Let pressure settle naturally for 3-4 min, then open.
  8. Add tomato + red chilli powder + dhania powder.
  9. Cook open for 3-4 min until tomato softens.
  10. Add ⅔ cup oats. Mix/roast for 1 min.
  11. Add remaining 1¼ cups water.
  12. Cook open on low-medium flame for 4-5 min, stirring often.
  13. Add extra splash of water if it gets too thick.
  14. Finish with garam masala pinch and lemon juice.

docs.title: Full pressure-cooker shortcut
docs.text:
Use only if you want a softer, more porridge-like texture.

  1. Follow steps 1-4 above.
  2. Add tomato + spices and cook 2-3 min.
  3. Add ⅔ cup oats + 1¾ cups water.
  4. Mix well, scrape bottom once.
  5. Close cooker and cook on low-medium flame for 1 whistle only.
  6. Switch off immediately.
  7. Let pressure release naturally for 5 min, then open.
  8. Mix well. Add hot water if too thick.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Prefer the recommended method, not the full shortcut — oats cook fast and taste better when cooked open after the potato is done.
  • Monsoon note: if not eating immediately, cool and refrigerate within 30-40 min as per the plan. Never freeze — the potato rules this out anyway.

docs.title: Matar Paneer
docs.text: Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: 1 day · Freezer-safe: no Morning active time: 20 min · Serves: 1 person, lunch + dinner Uses prepped components: onion-tomato masala base — 150 g / 4 heaped tbsp Source: Hebbars Kitchen Matar Paneer, adapted for prepped masala base

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Paneer 200 g, cubes (150 g is sufficient on non-gym weeks) Frozen peas ¾ cup Onion-tomato masala base 150 g / 4 heaped tbsp Oil or ghee 2 tsp Jeera ½ tsp Ginger-garlic paste ½ tsp, optional Haldi ¼ tsp Red chilli powder ½ tsp Dhania powder ½ tsp Garam masala ½ tsp Kasuri methi ½ tsp, crushed Salt ½ tsp or to taste Water ¾ cup Cream 1 tbsp, optional Coriander 1 tbsp, optional

docs.title: Method
docs.text:
1. If paneer is store-bought and firm, soak cubes in warm water for 5 min, then drain.
  2. Heat oil or ghee in kadai. Add jeera and ginger-garlic paste. Sauté 30 sec.
  3. Add onion-tomato masala base, haldi, red chilli powder, dhania powder, and salt. Cook 3-4 min.
  4. Add frozen peas and ¾ cup water. Cover and simmer 6-7 min.
  5. Add paneer cubes and kasuri methi. Simmer gently for 3-4 min only.
  6. Add garam masala and optional cream. Mix once and switch off.

docs.title: Portion & Store
docs.text:
• Split into 2 portions: lunch + dinner.
  • Cool 30-40 min, then refrigerate the dinner portion.
  • Reheat gently in kadai with 1-2 tbsp water; avoid hard boiling after paneer is added.
  • Eat same day; do not freeze.
  • Buy fresh paneer weekly and use opened paneer within 2-3 days; do not keep opened paneer loosely in the fridge.

docs.title: What it becomes / variations
docs.text: • Add carrot-beans mix, but keep paneer and peas as the main bulk.

docs.title: Direct Pressure-Cooker Milk Daliya
docs.text:
Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: Same day; cook and eat fresh · Freezer-safe: No, because milk-based daliya may separate and become grainy after freezing Active time: 10-12 min · Makes: 1 dinner for 1 person Uses prepped components: none Source: own

Style: soft, creamy, mildly sweet wheat daliya cooked directly with milk and a little water.

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Daliya / broken wheat ½ cup, about 75-80 g Ghee 1 tsp Milk 1½ cups, about 375 ml Water ½ cup, about 125 ml Sugar 1-1½ tbsp, adjust to taste Elaichi powder ⅛ tsp, optional Almonds or cashews 1 tbsp, chopped, optional

docs.title: Process — Direct Pressure Cooker
docs.text:
1. Use a 3-litre or larger pressure cooker. The milk and daliya mixture must remain below half of the cooker's capacity so the foaming milk does not reach the vent.
  2. Heat 1 tsp ghee directly in the cooker over low-medium heat.
  3. Add ½ cup daliya and roast for 4-5 minutes, stirring continuously, until lightly aromatic. Do not brown it deeply.
  4. Reduce to low heat. Add ½ cup water gradually and stir thoroughly, scraping the bottom so no roasted daliya remains stuck.
  5. Add 1½ cups milk and mix well. Do not add the sugar yet, because sugar can increase sticking.
  6. Keep the cooker uncovered over low-medium heat for 2-3 minutes, stirring once or twice, until the liquid becomes hot but is not vigorously boiling.
  7. Stir once from the bottom, close the cooker, and fit the whistle.
  8. Cook over medium-low heat for 1 whistle. Immediately reduce to the lowest heat and cook for another 4 minutes.
  9. Switch off the heat and allow the pressure to release naturally. Do not force-release the pressure because the milk may still be foamy.
  10. Open carefully and stir thoroughly from the bottom. Add 1-1½ tbsp sugar and optional ⅛ tsp elaichi powder.
  11. If the daliya is thicker than preferred, add 2-4 tbsp hot milk and simmer uncovered over low heat for 1-2 minutes.
  12. Rest for 3 minutes before serving. Add the chopped nuts, if using.

docs.title: Portioning and Storage
docs.text:
• Eat the full quantity fresh as 1 dinner portion.
  • The daliya will thicken as it rests; loosen it with a little hot milk if needed.
  • If any portion remains, cool it to warm within 30-40 minutes, cover, and refrigerate immediately rather than leaving it at room temperature.
  • Reheat only the portion being eaten in a small kadai over low heat, adding 2-3 tbsp milk and stirring until piping hot throughout.
  • Do not freeze.

docs.title: Monsoon-Safe Sprouts (Steamed)
docs.text:
Type: weekend-prep Fridge life: 2 days (steamed) · Freezer-safe: no Active time: 2-day timeline, ~10 min active + 8-10 min steam · Makes: half cup dry moong/moth Uses prepped components: idli pot for steaming Source: own, safety-validated

Raw sprouts in monsoon = genuine food-poisoning risk. Always steam or sauté in this season.

Timeline (start 2 days before you want them):

  1. Day 0 night: pick and rinse ½ cup moong or moth well. Soak in plenty of water for 8 hrs.
  2. Day 1 morning: drain, rinse, and drain completely. Leave in a covered colander or sieve; avoid a very wet cloth in monsoon because it can turn slimy.
  3. Day 1 evening + Day 2 morning: rinse and drain fully again. The sprouts should smell fresh, not sour.
  4. Day 2: sprouts are ready when tails are ½-1 inch. If sticky, sour-smelling, or slimy, discard.
  5. Steam 8-10 min in your idli pot, or sauté covered for 7-8 min with 1-2 tbsp water, till hot throughout. Cool, fridge, use within 2 days.

Gym lunch bowl (5 min): steamed sprouts + chopped onion-tomato + green chilli + curd + chaat masala + lemon + optional boiled potato cubes. Add curd only after sprouts cool to warm/room temperature. Protein depends on curd quantity; use extra curd if this is a gym meal.

docs.title: One-Pot Masala Fusilli Pasta — 1 person, 1 meal
docs.text:
Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: 1 day best in monsoon · Freezer-safe: No Active time: 20-25 min (morning) · Makes: 1 meal portion Uses prepped components: none — fresh one-pot dish Source: own Kitchen-tested: ✅ Yes

Best method is the steel pan with glass lid. The pressure cooker works too, but pasta can become softer.

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Fusilli pasta 85 g (≈1-1.1 cups loose); weigh — cup volume varies by shape/brand Oil or butter 1 tbsp Garlic 3-4 cloves, finely chopped Onion 1 small, chopped Tomato 1 medium, chopped or grated Green chilli 1, slit/chopped Capsicum / frozen peas-carrot-beans ¼ cup, optional Salt ¾ tsp, adjust later Haldi 1 pinch, optional Red chilli powder ¼ tsp Black pepper ¼ tsp Mixed herbs / oregano ½ tsp, optional Tomato ketchup 1 tsp, optional Water 1¼ cups (pan) / 1 cup (cooker) Cheese 1-2 cubes grated or 2 tbsp grated

docs.title: Method 1: Steel pan with glass lid — recommended
docs.text:
1. Heat 1 tbsp oil/butter in the steel pan.
  2. Add chopped garlic + green chilli. Sauté for 30 seconds.
  3. Add chopped onion. Cook for 2-3 minutes till slightly soft.
  4. Add chopped/grated tomato.
  5. Add salt, red chilli powder, black pepper, optional haldi, and optional mixed herbs.
  6. Cook for 3-4 minutes till tomato becomes soft and saucy.
  7. Add optional frozen peas-carrot-beans/capsicum. Cook for 1 minute.
  8. Add fusilli pasta.
  9. Add 1¼ cups water and mix well.
  10. Cover with glass lid.
  11. Cook on medium-low flame for 10-12 minutes, stirring every 3-4 minutes so it does not stick.
  12. Once pasta is cooked and sauce is thick, switch off flame.
  13. Rest covered for 2 minutes.
  14. Add grated cheese while pasta is hot.
  15. Mix lightly and serve.

docs.title: Method 2: Pressure cooker one-pot pasta
docs.text:
1. Heat 1 tbsp oil/butter in the pressure cooker.
  2. Add chopped garlic + green chilli. Sauté for 30 seconds.
  3. Add chopped onion. Cook for 2-3 minutes till slightly soft.
  4. Add chopped/grated tomato.
  5. Add salt, red chilli powder, black pepper, optional haldi, and optional mixed herbs.
  6. Cook for 3-4 minutes till tomato becomes soft and saucy.
  7. Add optional frozen peas-carrot-beans/capsicum. Cook for 1 minute.
  8. Add fusilli pasta.
  9. Add 1 cup water and mix well. Pasta should be just about covered, not drowned.
  10. Close the pressure cooker lid.
  11. Cook on medium flame for 1 whistle only.
  12. Switch off flame immediately after 1 whistle.
  13. Wait 3-4 minutes, then release pressure carefully.
  14. Open and mix gently.
  15. If it looks watery, simmer open for 1-2 minutes.
  16. Switch off flame.
  17. Add grated cheese while pasta is hot.
  18. Mix lightly and serve.

docs.title: Cheese timing
docs.text:
Add cheese after cooking, while serving hot.

Do not add cheese during pressure cooking or while boiling because it can stick, split, or make the pasta heavy. For pan method also, adding at the end gives better texture.

docs.title: If making lunch + dinner same day
docs.text:
Double everything:

Ingredient Quantity Fusilli pasta 170 g (≈2-2.2 cups loose) Water 2¼-2½ cups (pan) / 2 cups (cooker)

Cool within 30-40 minutes, refrigerate, and reheat thoroughly before dinner.

docs.title: One-Pot Thin Dal Sambar for Idli
docs.text:
Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: 3 days · Freezer-safe: Yes, up to 2 weeks Active time: 12-15 min · Makes: 325-350 ml; 1 dinner for 1 person Uses prepped components: none Source: own

Style: thin, pourable and mildly tangy sambar for 6 idlis, cooked entirely in one pressure cooker.

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Arhar dal 1½ tbsp, about 20 g Moong dhuli dal 1 tbsp, about 13 g Moong chhilka dal 1 tsp, about 5 g Water 250 ml for pressure cooking + 75-100 ml later Oil 1 tsp Rai ½ tsp Jeera ¼ tsp Hing 1 pinch Dry red chilli 1, broken, optional Curry leaves 5-6, optional but recommended Haldi ⅛ tsp Sambar masala 1¼ tsp Tamarind paste ½ tsp Salt ½ tsp, adjust to taste Jaggery or sugar ⅛ tsp, optional

docs.title: Process — One-Pot Pressure Cooker
docs.text:
1. Wash all three dals together 2-3 times, then drain.
  2. Heat 1 tsp oil in the open pressure cooker over medium-low heat.
  3. Add ½ tsp rai. Once it crackles, add ¼ tsp jeera, 1 pinch hing, the optional dry red chilli and 5-6 curry leaves.
  4. Fry for 20-30 seconds without letting the jeera, hing or curry leaves burn.
  5. Add the washed dals and ⅛ tsp haldi. Stir for 20-30 seconds to coat the dals with the tempering.
  6. Add 250 ml water and scrape the bottom thoroughly so nothing remains stuck.
  7. Close the cooker and cook over medium-high heat until the first whistle. Reduce to low heat and cook for another 6 minutes.
  8. Switch off the heat and allow the pressure to release naturally.
  9. Open the cooker and mash the cooked dals thoroughly with a spoon or whisk.
  10. Add ½ tsp tamarind paste, 1¼ tsp sambar masala, ½ tsp salt and the optional ⅛ tsp jaggery or sugar. Do not add the tamarind before pressure cooking because its acidity can slow the softening of the dal.
  11. Add 75 ml hot water, mix well and simmer uncovered in the same cooker over medium-low heat for 6-8 minutes, stirring occasionally.
  12. Add up to 25 ml more hot water if required. The finished sambar should be thin and easily pourable, with approximately 325-350 ml total volume.
  13. Taste and adjust the salt or tamarind. Serve piping hot with 6 idlis and coconut chutney.

docs.title: Portioning and Storage
docs.text:
• This quantity is intended for one dinner and can be consumed completely with the idlis.
  • If storing any remainder, cool it to warm within 30-40 minutes, cover and refrigerate.
  • Refrigerated sambar keeps for up to 3 days and is freezer-safe for up to 2 weeks.
  • Reheat only the portion being eaten in a small kadai or the pressure-cooker base.
  • Add 1-2 tbsp water if it has thickened and bring it to a bubbling simmer before serving.

docs.title: Onion Green Chilli Poha — 1 person lunch
docs.text: Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: best eaten fresh; can refrigerate 6-8 hours if needed · Freezer-safe: No Active time: 15-18 min (morning) · Makes: 1 lunch portion Uses prepped components: none Source: own Kitchen-tested: ✅ Yes

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Poha 1 cup / 80-90 g, thick poha Onion 1 small, finely chopped Green chilli 1, chopped Peanuts 1.5 tbsp, optional but recommended Oil 1.5 tbsp Mustard seeds ½ tsp Jeera ¼ tsp, optional Curry leaves 6-8, optional Hing 1 pinch Haldi ¼ tsp Salt ¾ tsp, adjust Sugar ½ tsp, optional Lemon juice 1-1.5 tsp Fresh coriander optional Water only for rinsing/sprinkling

docs.title: Process
docs.text:
1. Put poha in a sieve. Rinse gently under running water for 5-7 seconds only.
  2. Keep aside for 8-10 min to soften. Fluff with fingers. It should be soft but not mushy.
  3. Heat oil in kadai.
  4. Add peanuts. Fry on low-medium till slightly crisp. Remove and keep aside, or keep in kadai.
  5. Add mustard seeds. Let crackle.
  6. Add jeera, hing, curry leaves, green chilli.
  7. Add chopped onion + pinch of salt. Cook 3-4 min till onion turns soft, not fully brown.
  8. Add haldi, mix for 10 seconds.
  9. Add softened poha, salt, sugar. Mix gently.
  10. Sprinkle 1-2 tbsp water, cover, cook on low flame for 2-3 min.
  11. Switch off. Add lemon juice and peanuts. Mix gently.
  12. Rest covered for 2 min, then eat.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Use thick poha, not thin poha.
  • Since this skips potato/tomato/veggies, onion + peanuts are important for body and taste.
  • For a fuller lunch, have with curd or lehsun chutney/achaar on the side — the pantry system already keeps poha, curd, achaar, and lehsun chutney stocked.

docs.title: Onion-Tomato Masala Base
docs.text:
Type: weekend-prep Fridge life: 4 days · Freezer-safe: yes (3-4 weeks) Active time: ~40 min (mostly passive stirring) · Makes: makes 6-7 single-meal portions Uses prepped components: none Source: own, spice-blooming technique from Hebbars Kitchen curry base

The single highest-ROI item. Every weekday gravy starts from here.

Makes: ~600-700 g = 6-7 single-meal portions | Time: ~40 min, mostly passive stirring

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Onions 500 g, finely chopped or thinly sliced (5-6 medium) Tomatoes 600 g, chopped or pulsed coarse (6-7 medium) Ginger-garlic paste 2 tbsp (or 8 garlic cloves + 1 inch ginger, crushed) Oil 4 tbsp Jeera 1 tsp Salt 1 tsp, divided Haldi 1 tsp Red chilli powder 1.5 tsp Dhania powder 1 tbsp Water 2-3 tbsp, only for blooming dry spices

docs.title: Method
docs.text:
1. Heat oil in your biggest kadai. Add jeera and let it splutter.
  2. Add onions + ½ tsp salt. Sauté on medium till deep golden — 12-15 min. Don't rush this; the colour is the flavour.
  3. Add ginger-garlic paste, sauté 1-2 min till raw smell goes.
  4. Lower the flame. Add haldi, chilli, dhania, and 2-3 tbsp water. Stir 30-45 sec so the spices bloom without burning.
  5. Add tomatoes and remaining salt. Cook on medium, stirring occasionally, till completely mushy, thick, and oil separates at the edges — 15-18 min.
  6. Cool fully.

docs.title: Portion & Store
docs.text:
• 2-3 portions (about 100 g / 2 heaped tbsp each) → fridge box, use within 4 days
  • Rest → freezer in small 200 ml containers or a silicone cube tray (2 cubes ≈ 1 portion). Keeps 3-4 weeks.
  • To use from frozen: drop the frozen block straight into the hot kadai with a splash of water. No defrosting needed.

docs.title: What it becomes / variations
docs.text: • rajma, chole, aloo-mangodi, matar-paneer-style gravies, dal tadka upgrade, veg curry, even quick pav-bhaji-ish mash with boiled potato.

docs.title: Pav Bhaji
docs.text: Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: 1 day · Freezer-safe: no Morning active time: 25 min · Serves: 1 person, lunch + dinner Uses prepped components: onion-tomato masala base — 150 g / 4 heaped tbsp; boiled potatoes — 250 g Source: Vidhya’s Vegetarian Kitchen Pav Bhaji, adapted as fridge-only version

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Boiled potatoes 250 g / 3 medium, peeled and mashed Onion-tomato masala base 150 g / 4 heaped tbsp Frozen peas-carrot-beans mix 1 cup Gobi 1 cup small florets, optional Capsicum ½ cup, chopped, optional, only if bought that week Oil 2 tsp Butter 1 tsp + extra only while serving Ginger-garlic paste ½ tsp, optional Pav bhaji masala 1½ tsp Red chilli powder ½ tsp Haldi ¼ tsp Salt ¾ tsp or to taste Water ¾ to 1 cup Lemon juice 1-2 tsp Onion 2 tbsp, finely chopped, optional garnish Coriander 1 tbsp, optional

docs.title: Method
docs.text:
1. Heat oil and 1 tsp butter in kadai.
  2. Add ginger-garlic paste and sauté 30 sec.
  3. Add onion-tomato masala base, pav bhaji masala, red chilli powder, haldi, and salt. Cook 3-4 min.
  4. Add frozen veg, gobi if using, capsicum if using, and ¾ cup water.
  5. Cover and cook 8-10 min till vegetables are soft.
  6. Add mashed boiled potatoes.
  7. Mash everything together with a potato masher. Add more water if too thick.
  8. Simmer 5-7 min till bhaji becomes thick and uniform.
  9. Finish with lemon juice, coriander, and a small butter touch only while serving.

docs.title: Portion & Store
docs.text:
• Split into 2 portions: lunch + dinner.
  • Cool 30-40 min, then refrigerate the dinner portion.
  • Fridge-only because this uses potato. Eat within the same day; max next-day lunch if smell and texture are perfect.
  • Never freeze pav bhaji in this system.

docs.title: What it becomes / variations
docs.text: • Eat with 3 rotis per meal, dosa, or fresh pav; do not mix into stored rice.

docs.title: Plain-Boiled Dal + Fresh Tadka Method
docs.text:
Type: weekend-prep Fridge life: 3 days · Freezer-safe: yes (2-3 weeks) Active time: 15 min (passive) + 3 min daily tadka · Makes: 1 cup dry = 3-4 meals Uses prepped components: none Source: own, validated

The restaurant trick: dal cooked ahead, tadka fresh daily. Fresh tadka makes 3-day-old dal taste made-today.

Batch: 1 cup toor or moong dal = 3-4 meals | Time: 15 min, passive

docs.title: Method
docs.text:
1. Rinse dal 3-4 times till water runs mostly clear.
  2. Pressure cook with 3 cups water, ½ tsp salt, ¼ tsp haldi, and ½ tsp oil or ghee.
  3. Toor dal: 3-4 whistles
  4. Moong dal: 2-3 whistles
  5. Let pressure release naturally.
  6. Open and whisk/mash once while warm. Add a little hot water only if too thick.
  7. Cool. Store: 2 portions fridge (3 days), rest freezer.
