Daily tadka (3 min): for 1 day of lunch + dinner, heat 2 tsp ghee/oil → add 1 tsp jeera, hing, 1 chopped green chilli, optional garlic/curry leaves → add 2 portions boiled dal + ¼ to ½ cup water → simmer 3-4 min → finish. For dal tadka deluxe: bloom the tadka in 1 tbsp of masala base first.

docs.title: Plain-Boiled Legumes — Rajma / Chole / Chana / Moth
docs.text:
Type: weekend-prep Fridge life: 3 days · Freezer-safe: yes (2-3 weeks) Active time: ~30 min per batch (passive cooker time) · Makes: 1 cup dry = 3 meals Uses prepped components: none Source: own, validated

Boil plain on the weekend; add masala fresh on the day. Plain-boiled = flexible.

Batch size: 1 cup dry per variety = ~2.5 cups boiled = 3 meals. Boil 2 varieties per week.

docs.title: Method
docs.text:
1. Saturday night (or 8-12 hrs before prep): soak each variety separately in plenty of water. Keep the bowl loosely covered, not airtight.
  2. Prep day: drain, rinse well, and add to the pressure cooker with fresh water, ½ tsp salt, and ¼ tsp haldi.
  3. Rajma: 1 cup dry rajma (measured before soaking) + 3 cups water → 5-6 whistles, then low flame 10 min
  4. Chole/chana: 1 cup dry chole/chana (measured before soaking) + 3 cups water → 5-6 whistles
  5. Moth/moong (whole): 1 cup dry moth/moong (measured before soaking) + 2.5 cups water → 2-3 whistles
  6. Let pressure release naturally.
  7. Check doneness: rajma/chole/chana should mash easily between fingers but still hold shape. If firm, pressure cook 1-2 whistles more.
  8. Cool in the cooker. Store with the cooking liquid — it keeps them moist and the liquid enriches the gravy later.

docs.title: Portion & Store
docs.text:
• First 3 days' portions → fridge (glass box)
  • Rest → freezer with liquid, flat containers. Keeps 2-3 weeks.

On the day (10-12 min): heat 1 portion masala base in kadai → add boiled legumes with their liquid → garam masala / chole masala / amchur as suits → simmer 8-10 min → done. Same boiled chana also works as a quick chaat (see Variation 3).

docs.title: Plain Moong Dal — Dhuli + Chhilka Mix
docs.text:
Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: same day best; dinner portion safe if cooled and refrigerated within 30-40 min · Freezer-safe: Yes, but not needed for same day Active time: 15-20 min (morning) · Makes: 2 bowls dal = lunch + dinner for 1 person Uses prepped components: none Source: own Kitchen-tested: ✅ Yes

Mix ratio: 70% dhuli moong + 30% moong chilka. Pairs with 6 rotis + aloo pyaaz tamatar sabzi. Make it slightly thin because moong dal thickens by dinner.

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Dhuli moong dal ⅓ cup Moong chilka dal 2 tbsp Water 2.25 to 2.5 cups Salt ¾ tsp, adjust Haldi ¼ tsp Ghee or oil 1 tsp Jeera ¾ tsp Hing 1 pinch optional Green chilli 1 slit optional Ginger ½ tsp grated optional Lemon few drops optional, add while eating

docs.title: Process
docs.text:
1. Wash both dals 3-4 times until water is mostly clear.
  2. Soak for 10 minutes if you have time. Optional, but helps it cook evenly.

  3. Add dal to pressure cooker with:

    • 2.25 to 2.5 cups water
    • ¼ tsp haldi
    • ¾ tsp salt

  4. Pressure cook:

    • 2 whistles on medium flame
    • Then switch off and let pressure release naturally for 5-10 min.

  5. Open and whisk lightly with a spoon. Keep it slightly loose. Add hot water if too thick.

  6. For tadka, heat 1 tsp ghee/oil in small tadka pan.
  7. Add jeera + hing + green chilli + ginger.
  8. Let jeera crackle, then pour tadka into dal.
  9. Simmer dal for 2-3 min after tadka.
  10. Taste salt. Add lemon only in your bowl while eating, not in the whole dal if storing for dinner.

docs.title: Storage for dinner
docs.text:
• Take out dinner portion before eating lunch.
  • Cool 30-40 min max, then refrigerate covered.
  • Reheat in a small pan with 2-4 tbsp water because it will thicken by evening.
  • Bring it to a proper boil before eating.

docs.title: Potato Pulao (One-Pot)
docs.text:
Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: 1 day · Freezer-safe: no Morning active time: 25 min · Serves: 1 person, lunch + dinner Uses prepped components: home curd, on the side Source: own, one-pot pressure-cooker potato pulao

One-pot rice + potato meal. Because potato is already here and you'll eat it with curd, ¾ cup raw rice is the moderate portion for lunch + dinner. Use 1 cup raw rice only if you want a heavy quantity or you eat more rice.

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Raw rice ¾ cup / about 140-150 g Water 1½ cups for pressure cooker Potato 1 medium/large, diced small Onion 1 medium/large, sliced/chopped Tomato 1 medium, chopped Green chilli 1-2 Oil or ghee 2 tbsp Cumin seeds ¾ tsp Hing 1 pinch, optional Ginger ½-1 tsp grated, optional Turmeric ⅓ tsp Red chilli powder ¼-½ tsp Coriander powder ¾ tsp Garam masala ¼ tsp, optional Salt 1 to 1¼ tsp, adjust as per taste Fresh coriander optional

docs.title: Method
docs.text:
1. Wash ¾ cup rice 2-3 times. Soak for 10 minutes if possible, then drain.
  2. Heat 2 tbsp oil/ghee in the pressure cooker.
  3. Add ¾ tsp cumin seeds and a pinch of hing.
  4. Add onion + green chilli. Cook for 3-4 minutes until onion becomes light pink.
  5. Add potato. Cook for 3-4 minutes on medium flame.
  6. Add tomato and cook for 2 minutes.
  7. Add the dry spices: ⅓ tsp turmeric, ¼-½ tsp red chilli powder, ¾ tsp coriander powder, 1 to 1¼ tsp salt, and ¼ tsp garam masala (optional).
  8. Cook the masala for 2-3 minutes, until tomato becomes soft.
  9. Add the drained rice. Mix gently for 30-40 seconds.
  10. Add 1½ cups water. Mix once.
  11. Pressure cook on medium flame for 1 whistle, then switch off.
  12. Let pressure release naturally for 8-10 minutes. Open and fluff gently. Garnish with fresh coriander if using.

docs.title: Portion & Store
docs.text:
• Split into 2 portions: lunch + dinner.
  • After lunch, spread the dinner portion on a wide plate/bowl for 15-20 minutes to cool, then keep it in an airtight box in the fridge.
  • For dinner, reheat with 1-2 tbsp water until steaming hot. Eat within the same day.
  • Never freeze — it has both rice and potato. Serve with home curd.

docs.title: What it becomes / variations
docs.text:
• Richer version: bloom the spices in 1 tbsp onion-tomato masala base before adding the rice.
  • Veg pulao: add ½ cup frozen peas/carrot along with the potato.

docs.title: Sambar-Style Dal (the Idli Companion)
docs.text:
Type: weekend-prep Fridge life: 3 days · Freezer-safe: yes (2 weeks) Active time: 25 min · Makes: 4 portions Uses prepped components: chopped veg box or frozen veg Source: own, validated

80% of sambar at 30% of the effort.

Batch: 1 cup toor dal version = 4 portions | Time: 25 min

  1. Rinse 1 cup toor dal well. Pressure cook with 3 cups water, ¼ tsp haldi, ½ tsp oil, chopped lauki or frozen carrot/beans, and 1 chopped tomato — 4 whistles. Let pressure release naturally.
  2. Open and mash the dal lightly. Add 1.5-2 tbsp sambar powder, small lemon-sized tamarind soaked and squeezed (or 2 tsp tamarind paste), salt, jaggery pinch, and 1-1.5 cups water as needed. Simmer 8-10 min.
  3. Tadka: heat 2 tsp oil, add ½ tsp mustard seeds, hing, curry leaves, and 1 dry red chilli. Pour into the simmered sambar-style dal and cover 2 min.

Store: 2 portions fridge, 2 freezer. Idli day = steam idlis from readymade batter (12-15 min, passive) + reheat this = a real 15-minute meal. Batter keeps 3-4 days in fridge; buy it fresh with your weekly shop.

docs.title: Soya Bhurji
docs.text: Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: 1 day · Freezer-safe: no Morning active time: 25 min · Serves: 1 person, lunch + dinner Uses prepped components: onion-tomato masala base — 120 g / 3 heaped tbsp Source: My Tasty Curry Soya Bhurji, adapted for stovetop and prepped masala base

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Soya granules / Nutrela granules 70 g / about ¾ cup Hot water enough to soak Salt ½ tsp for soaking + ¼ tsp for cooking Oil 2 tsp Jeera ½ tsp Green chilli 1, chopped Ginger ½ inch, grated Onion-tomato masala base 120 g / 3 heaped tbsp Frozen peas ½ cup Capsicum ½ cup, finely chopped, optional, only if bought that week Haldi ¼ tsp Red chilli powder ½ tsp Garam masala ½ tsp Lemon juice 1 tsp Coriander 1 tbsp, optional

docs.title: Method
docs.text:
1. Soak soya granules in hot salted water for 10 min.
  2. Drain, rinse once, squeeze hard with your hands to remove excess water. Keep aside.
  3. Heat oil in kadai. Add jeera, green chilli, and ginger. Sauté 30 sec.
  4. Add onion-tomato masala base and cook 2-3 min till hot and slightly thick.
  5. Add peas, capsicum if using, haldi, red chilli powder, garam masala, and 2-3 tbsp water. Cook 4-5 min.
  6. Add squeezed soya granules and salt. Mix well.
  7. Cook 6-8 min on medium-low, stirring often, till bhurji becomes dry but not hard.
  8. Finish with lemon juice and coriander.

docs.title: Portion & Store
docs.text:
• Split into 2 portions: lunch + dinner.
  • Cool 30-40 min, then refrigerate the dinner portion.
  • Reheat on kadai with 1-2 tbsp water; eat within the same day.
  • Do not freeze finished soya bhurji in this system.

docs.title: What it becomes / variations
docs.text: • Use as roti filling, sandwich filling, or mix with leftover fresh rice only if rice is same-day cooked.

docs.title: Standalone Gawar Masala Sabji
docs.text:
Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: 2-3 days; refrigerate the dinner portion after cooling 30-40 min · Freezer-safe: No, because cooked gawar becomes excessively soft and watery after thawing Active time: 30-35 min (morning) · Makes: 2 meals for 1 person Uses prepped components: none Source: own

Style: semi-dry gawar-only sabji with simple masalas. Pairs with 3 rotis per meal; curd or dal can be served alongside.

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Gawar fali 450 g before trimming Oil 1½ tbsp Jeera ½ tsp Ajwain ¼ tsp Hing 1 generous pinch Green chilli 1-2, finely chopped Haldi ¼ tsp Red chilli powder ½ tsp, adjust to taste Dhania powder 1½ tsp Jeera powder ½ tsp Salt ¾ tsp, or to taste Water ¼ cup, about 60 ml Amchur ¾ tsp Garam masala ¼ tsp, optional Jaggery or sugar ¼ tsp, optional; balances bitterness

docs.title: Process
docs.text:
1. Wash the gawar fali thoroughly and drain it. Remove the stalk ends and pull away any tough strings along the sides.
  2. Chop the gawar into approximately 2 cm pieces.
  3. Heat 1½ tbsp oil in a kadai over medium heat.
  4. Add ½ tsp jeera and ¼ tsp ajwain. Once they crackle, add 1 generous pinch hing.
  5. Add the chopped green chilli and sauté for 20-30 seconds.
  6. Reduce the heat to low. Add ¼ tsp haldi, ½ tsp red chilli powder, 1½ tsp dhania powder, and ½ tsp jeera powder. Stir for only 10-15 seconds so the spices do not burn.
  7. Immediately add the chopped gawar and ¾ tsp salt. Mix thoroughly so every piece is coated with the oil and masalas.
  8. Sauté uncovered over medium heat for 4-5 minutes, stirring frequently.
  9. Add ¼ cup water and mix. Cover the kadai and cook over low-medium heat for 16-20 minutes.
  10. Open and stir every 4-5 minutes. If the kadai becomes completely dry before the gawar is tender, add only 1-2 tbsp water at a time.
  11. Check one of the thicker pieces. The gawar should be completely tender without becoming mashed. If it remains fibrous, cover and cook for another 4-5 minutes.
  12. Once tender, remove the lid and cook over medium heat for 3-5 minutes, stirring regularly, until all excess moisture evaporates.
  13. Add ¾ tsp amchur, optional ¼ tsp garam masala, and optional ¼ tsp jaggery or sugar.
  14. Mix and cook uncovered for 1-2 minutes. Taste and adjust the salt or amchur.

docs.title: Portioning and Storage
docs.text:
• Divide the sabji into 2 equal portions, one each for lunch and dinner.
  • Each portion pairs with 3 rotis. Since this is a gawar-only sabji, curd or plain dal can be served alongside for a fuller meal.
  • Remove the dinner portion from the hot kadai and let it cool for 30-40 minutes.
  • Transfer it to a covered container and refrigerate immediately. Do not leave it at room temperature until evening.
  • At dinner, reheat only the portion being eaten in a kadai with 1-2 tbsp water.
  • Cover and reheat over low-medium heat for 4-5 minutes, stirring once or twice, until piping hot throughout.
  • Do not freeze this sabji.

docs.title: Steamed Idli
docs.text:
Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: Same day; refrigerate the dinner portion after cooling 30-40 min · Freezer-safe: No, because freezing is outside the current storage plan and can make reheated idlis dry Active time: 15-20 min (morning) · Makes: 2 meals for 1 person, about 8 medium idlis Uses prepped components: none Source: own

Style: soft steamed idlis. Serve about 4 idlis per meal with sambar/dal, coconut chutney, or podi.

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Readymade idli/dosa batter 450-500 g, about 2 cups Salt ⅛-¼ tsp, only if the batter needs it Oil ½ tsp, for greasing the moulds Water 2-2½ cups, for steaming

docs.title: Process
docs.text:
1. Remove 450-500 g batter from the refrigerator and mix it gently for 15-20 seconds. Do not beat vigorously.
  2. Check the consistency. The batter should be thick but pourable. If it is unusually thick, mix in 1 tbsp water at a time, using no more than 2 tbsp unless required.
  3. Taste a tiny amount and add ⅛-¼ tsp salt only if the readymade batter needs it.
  4. Add 2-2½ cups water to the idli pot. Keep the water level below the lowest idli tray.
  5. Cover and bring the water to a strong boil over high heat for 4-5 minutes.
  6. Meanwhile, grease 8 idli moulds lightly with approximately ½ tsp oil in total.
  7. Fill each mould approximately ¾ full with batter. Do not fill to the rim.
  8. Once the water is boiling, place the filled trays in the pot and cover immediately.
  9. Steam over medium-high heat for 10-12 minutes. Do not open the lid during the first 10 minutes.
  10. Insert a toothpick or knife into the centre of one idli. If wet batter sticks to it, steam for another 2 minutes.
  11. Switch off the heat and leave the pot covered for 2 minutes.
  12. Remove the trays and rest them for 3-4 minutes. Loosen each idli with a wet spoon or knife and remove gently.

docs.title: Portioning and Storage
docs.text:
• Divide the idlis into 2 portions of about 4 idlis each, one for lunch and one for dinner.
  • Remove the dinner portion from the hot tray and let it cool for 30-40 minutes.
  • Transfer it to a covered container and refrigerate immediately.
  • Store chutney, sambar, or dal separately according to its own storage rules.
  • At dinner, reheat only the portion being eaten.
  • To reheat, place the idlis on a greased steel plate or steaming tray above a small amount of boiling water.
  • Cover and steam over medium heat for 4-5 minutes, until piping hot throughout.
  • Do not repeatedly cool and reheat the same idlis.
  • Do not freeze the cooked idlis.

docs.title: Ven/Kara Pongal
docs.text: Type: weekday-dish Fridge life: same day only · Freezer-safe: no Morning active time: 25 min · Serves: 1 person, lunch + dinner Uses prepped components: boiled moong dal — 1 cup cooked Source: Hebbars Kitchen Ven Pongal / Khara Pongal, adapted for boiled-dal prep

docs.title: Ingredients
docs.text: Ingredient Quantity Raw rice ¾ cup Boiled moong dal 1 cup cooked Water 3 to 3½ cups Salt ¾ tsp or to taste Ghee 2 tsp Jeera 1 tsp Black pepper ¾ tsp, crushed Ginger 1 inch, finely chopped or grated Green chilli 1, slit, optional Hing 1 pinch Curry leaves 8-10, optional Cashews 8-10, optional Turmeric 1 pinch, optional for kara pongal style

docs.title: Method
docs.text:
1. Wash rice 2-3 times.
  2. Add rice, boiled moong dal, water, salt, and turmeric if using to the pressure cooker.
  3. Pressure cook for 3 whistles on medium flame. Let pressure release naturally.
  4. Open and mash lightly. Add hot water if needed for soft pongal consistency.
  5. Heat ghee in a small kadai.
  6. Add cashews if using and fry till light golden. Remove or keep in pan.
  7. Add jeera, crushed black pepper, ginger, green chilli, hing, and curry leaves. Fry 30-45 sec.
  8. Pour tadka into pongal and mix well.
  9. Simmer 2 min on low flame. Adjust salt and consistency.

docs.title: Portion & Store
docs.text:
• Split into 2 portions: lunch + dinner.
  • This is a rice-based same-day dish for days when both meals are eaten from the morning cook.
  • Cool the dinner portion for 30-40 min, then refrigerate.
  • Reheat dinner thoroughly in kadai with a splash of hot water.
  • Do not keep beyond the same day; do not freeze.

docs.title: What it becomes / variations
docs.text: • Add extra pepper for kara pongal; serve with curd, pickle, or sambar-style dal if available.
